EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the overnight hours on February 2nd and continued into midday.  Temperatures were in the teens which when combined with northeast winds of 20 to 30 mph caused reduced visibility and blowing and drifting of the dry powdery snow.  Parts of central and east central Kansas received 4 to 7 inches of snow from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KVOE radio station measured 5 inches of snow in Emporia.  North winds of 20 to 30 mph caused widespread blowing and drifting of snow especially outside of the city.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of CoCoRaHS, COOP and NWS reports measured or estimated around 5 inches of snow across the county.   North winds of 20 to 30 mph caused widespread blowing and drifting of the snow.  NWS employees measured between 4.5 and 5 inches of snow and the official NWS Topeka observation measured near 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Based solely on wind chill values, this six-county North Central Kansas area endured one of its overall most significant, but brief cold blasts of the last 30+ years during these days shortly before Christmas, with the harshest wind chills focused between the evening of the 21st and the morning of the 23rd. Based on observations from the AWOS at Smith Center Municipal Airport, which reasonably represented conditions across the area as a whole, most of North Central Kansas endured roughly 14 hours of extreme/rare wind chills around -30 degrees (F) or colder (Warning criteria) and around 40 straight hours of wind chills around -20 or colder (Advisory criteria). The absolute worst wind chills occurred on the morning of the 22nd, during which time nearly the entire area saw values plummet into the  -35 to -40 range, including semi-official extremes as low as -40 at Smith Center Airport (AWOS), -39 near Jewell (KSU mesonet) and -38 near Phillipsburg (KSU mesonet). Although some places faced very similar wind chills less than two years prior (during the significant, more prolonged cold blast of mid-February 2021), at least parts of the area likely hadn't experienced wind chills this brutal in over 30 years...since the winter of 1989-90! Digging a bit deeper into the comparison between the sheer magnitude of the cold/wind chills between this Dec. 2022 Extreme Cold event and the aforementioned Feb. 2021 event, this one was certainly driven more by strong winds and less-so by extreme ambient air temperatures. In fact, actual low temperatures across most of North Central Kansas on the morning of the 22nd only bottomed out in the -10 to -15 range. As a result, the extreme wind chill values were significantly driven by relentless north-northwest winds. During the entire 14-hour period that the Smith Center AWOS observed wind chills of -30 or colder (centered between the late evening of the 21st and the afternoon of the 22nd), sustained speeds averaged 20-30 MPH, accompanied by frequent gusts at least 30-35 MPH. Although most of the area saw wind chills climb to slightly warmer than -20 by mid-day on the 23rd (due to the combination of slowly-rising temperatures and decreasing winds), at least sporadic wind chills as cold as -10 to -15 persisted from the night of the 23rd into the morning of the 24th, after which time the final Wind Chill Advisory was allowed to expire. Interestingly, this brief-but-intense cold blast was actually followed by a steady warm-up into and beyond Christmas itself. Again referencing Smith Center data (generally representative of North Central Kansas as a whole), the daily high temperatures bottomed out at -4 degrees on the 22nd, but then steadily recovered to 12 degrees on the 23rd, 26 on the 24th and 35 on the 25th. In fact, less than a week after the brutal cold of the 22nd, North Central Kansas highs soared into the 50s (and even isolated low 60s) on the 28th.||Although the bitterly-cold wind chills were certainly the main weather story in North Central Kansas between the 21st-24th, the early part of this time frame also featured one of the area's first semi-impactful winter precipitation events of the season. To kick things off, much of the daylight hours on the 21st brought a light mix of snow/freezing drizzle. However, the main event transpired during the overnight hours, as a west-southwest to east-northeast oriented band of moderate to briefly-heavy snow gradually marched across the area, developing slightly behind the leading edge of a powerful Arctic cold front. This snow band mainly impacted the area between 11 PM and 5 AM before departing off to the south-southeast. Although the heaviest snow only lasted around two hours at any given location, this was long enough to drop a widespread 1-2 (isolated slightly higher), with visibility commonly reduced to at least one-half mile at times thanks to strong north winds sustained 25-30 MPH/gusting up to around 45 MPH. Though falling snow exited North Central Kansas before daybreak on the 22nd, the combination of residual blowing snow and the aforementioned extreme cold/wind chills made for a most unpleasant weather situation for the morning commute. Briefly touching on the meteorological background behind this Extreme Cold event, the aforementioned sharp Arctic front blasted through the area from north-to-south during the evening of the 21st. During the day on the 22nd, the still strong (but slowly decreasing) winds were driven by a stout pressure gradient directed between a roughly 1011 millibar low centered over northeast AR/southeast MO (along the cold front) and a stout 1055 millibar high centered over MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously low wind chills occurred across the entire county. The lowest measured wind chills occurred on the 22nd and included: -37 degrees, recorded by a Kansas State Mesonet station located in Osborne, and -35 degrees, recorded by a home mesonet station in Downs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved over extreme Southwest Nebraska into Northwest Kansas during the early to mid afternoon hours with a line of storms that developed along the front. The only severe thunderstorm wind gust was near the town of Lyle, Kansas with a gust of 66 mph.
On July 8th, an evening severe thunderstorm developed in northwestern Kansas due to daytime heating. This thunderstorm produced a 63 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site recorded a 63 mph thunderstorm wind gust at its location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon to evening hours of July 26, 2022, strong to severe storms developed over Northern New Mexico and Southeast Colorado moving east to northeast into Western Kansas. As the storms moved over Southern Wichita County, they produced pea to nickel sized hail south-southwest of Leoti, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail reported. Most of the hail was pea to dime in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large high precipitation supercell formed near the Nebraska Kansas border and tracked south during the evening of June 11th producing widespread damaging straight line winds of 80-110 mph along with several small tornadoes.   The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey and storm chaser video and photos revealed a brief tornado just northeast of Marysville.  This tornado did damage to a barn and power pole short path.  The damage to a lumber shop south of highway 36 appears to have been related to a possible RFD surge south (straight line winds) and not this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms moved out of Nebraska during the evening of June 7th and evolved into a line of storms that accelerated southeast across central and northeast Kansas. At least 2 small tornadoes occurred along with widespread damaging straight line winds of 60 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey revealed a 50-75 yard path of concentrated damage done to several structures and a line of cedars that were topped off on the west side of the Big Blue River south of Marysville.  The damage path extended to the east side of the river where tree damage was noted in a focused area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage survey revealed a garage was destroyed on the south side of state line road with debris thrown north back into Nebraska (across the road).  A sporadic damage path moved southeast and ended around highway 77 where power poles were broken along with some tree limb damage.   This tornado moved south out of Nebraska and ended in Kansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another tornado path that was nearly indistinguishable from the widespread straight line wind damage that preceded it into the north side of Blue Rapids.  It appears that a tornado did damage along a path that traveled just north of a Georgia Pacific Plant along and just west of highway 77 where it derailed train cars then pushed south into the river bottoms snapping trees.  The track became very broad as it approached the river where it appears it transitioned to a damaging downburst surge of 100-105 mph straight line winds across the city of Blue Rapids with the worst damage on the west side of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed little from the previous day of high winds and blowing dust. The upper level disturbance continued to slide northeast over the Upper Midwest. High winds rounding the backside of the disturbance were able to reach the ground over Northwest Kansas. The peak gust was 70 MPH at the Goodland airport. Dust storms also occurred due to the high winds and dry ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No wind gusts of 58 MPH or greater were reported in the county.  However wind gusts of 58 MPH or greater occurred in surrounding counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon an ATV started a fire 20 miles south of Quinter near the Smokey Hill River.  The fire burned 700 acres.  Four different fire departments worked to extinguish the fire.  No buildings were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large fire was confined to only pastures.  Most of the fire was put out by 16:30 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits from the summer/early fall months continued into October, with severe drought (D2) continuing and even expanding into extreme drought (D3) for portions of far eastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the previous several months and continuing into October yielded severe (D2) to extreme (D3) across portions of far eastern Kansas. In Wyandotte County, October began with all of the county within D2 drought. By October 11th, the drought monitor showed drought worsening to D3 across all of the county, and D3 drought continued through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits in the summer and continuing into the fall led to severe drought developing across Miami County by October 4th and continuing through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the summer months and continuing into fall led to severe drought developing across Miami County. On October 4th, the drought monitor showed D2 drought building into the eastern half of Miami County, with this expanding further to include all of the county by mid October and continuing through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the summer months and continuing into fall led to severe drought developing across portions of eastern Kansas by October 18th and continuing through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the summer months and continuing into fall led to severe drought developing across Atchison County by October 18th and continuing through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout October 2022, portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties were assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the ninth consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Following encouraging September precipitation trends for much of the area (roughly three-fourths of locations had above normal Sept. rainfall), October was overall dry area-wide...see below for more Oct. precipitation details. Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics, October opened with the majority of the six counties under a varied mix of Moderate Drought (D1) and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions, with most of the D0 focused within Jewell, Mitchell and northern Smith counties. On the best-off side of things, much of central/southern Mitchell County was actually void of all drought categories whatsoever. However, of greatest concern were various swaths of D2 within the four western local counties, impacting: 1) much of the northern halves of Rooks/Osborne counties along with most of far southern Phillips/Smith counties...2) a narrow stripe across extreme southern Rooks/Osborne counties...3) a narrow swath through far western Phillips County. Over the course of the month, drought categories were actually stable/unchanged in most places (including all D2 areas). However, late in the month some one-category degradation occurred mainly within the eastern half of the area, as existing D0 within all of Smith/Osborne and most of Jewell counties degraded to D1, while those portions of Mitchell County void of all drought categories assumed D0. As a result, at month's end D0 was present across most of Mitchell and southeastern Jewell counties. ||Turning to October 2022 precipitation details, unfortunately not only did ALL of North Central Kansas receive below normal amounts, but around 90 percent of the area measured less than one-half of normal (generally less than 1 inch) and around one-third of the area tallied LESS THAN ONE-FOURTH OF NORMAL (generally less than 0.50). Overall, most locations measured somewhere between 0.35-1.00. On the worst off side of things were western portions of Phillips/Rooks counties, where paltry totals per NWS/CoCoRaHS observers included merely 0.15 in Logan (7th-driest October on record out of 72 years) and 0.20 near Webster State Park. On the wettest (but still below normal) side of things, some of the highest measured amounts focused within Jewell and southern Rooks counties, led by 1.03 near Plainville and 0.98 in Formoso. Concluding with a look at increasingly-concerning longer-term drought trends, the majority of North Central Kansas racked up cumulative precipitation deficits of 10-18 inches during the 19-month period between April 2021-Oct. 2022 (precipitation amounts generally 60-80% of normal). Versus normal, and despite official drought categories, one of the most dire totals of all during that 19-month stretch was actually in Beloit, where the NWS Coop Observer tallied 35.06 (17.90 below normal/66% of normal).

The drought status remained unchanged through October for Northwest Kansas.  The worst of the drought continued to be along and east of Highway 27 where a large area of D3 Extreme Drought was present.  Rawlins and Decatur counties continued in the D4 Exceptional Drought category.  Precipitation ranged from a hundredth of an inch to one and a half inches.  This was as much as two inches below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The precipitation for the month ranged from a quarter of an inch to an inch.  This was half an inch to two inches below normal.  Normal precipitation ranges from one to two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance in fast northwest upper level flow brought a narrow swath of light to moderate snow across the High Plains during the morning hours of January 5th, then shifted eastward into western portions of the forecast area around midday. A dry low level airmass limited snowfall coverage and intensity, initially, but persistent and focused lift allowed for accumulations to begin during the afternoon hours. Accumulating snow continued into the evening hours, mainly for areas along and southwest of a line from Smith Center to Beloit. Snow largely ended by midnight, but not before producing a swath of 2 to 6 inches, heaviest across Phillips County. This was a dry snow due to cold temperatures, but fortunately, wind speeds were light enough to preclude significant blowing and drifting snow. Impacts were fairly typical of winter weather advisory level events such as limited visibility during periods of moderate snow, along with snow covered roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts as high as 3 to 5 inches fell, with the highest amounts across the southwestern half of the county.

Accumulating snow continued into the evening hours, mainly for areas along and southwest of a line from Smith Center to Beloit. Snow largely ended by midnight, but not before producing a swath of 2 to 6 inches, heaviest across Phillips County. This was a dry snow due to cold temperatures, but fortunately, wind speeds were light enough to preclude significant blowing and drifting snow. Impacts were fairly typical of winter weather advisory level events such as limited visibility during periods of moderate snow, along with snow covered roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts as high as 1 to 3 inches fell, with the highest amounts across far southwestern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain moved across the region during the early morning hours of the start of the new year, 2022.   A band of snowfall moved across portions of central Kansas, with 3 to 5 inches, while a mix of sleet and snow fell across south central Kansas.  Southeast Kansas saw mainly freezing rain with ice accumulations of a few hundredths to a tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches fell across the county.  The Coop observer in Windom and Bridgeport measured one inch.  Law enforcement reported 2 inches in McPherson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic front pushed through during the afternoon setting the stage for the development of widespread snow.  Southwest flow aloft ahead of an upper level low aided in 1-7 inches of snow south to north across the CWA with northern counties seeing more with the enhanced PVA anomalies located there. Snow tapered off through the afternoon with the main parent low as it exited quickly to the northeast leading to clearing skies and dangerously cold temperatures overnight into Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was observed at Scott City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the area during the late morning into the early afternoon producing strong north wind gusts of 60-70 mph. This was amplified by the strong gradient and cold air advection that sent temperatures down through the 40s and 30s quickly behind the frontal passage. High wind warnings were issued for nearly the entire CWA during the afternoon and evening hours. Winds diminished to 45-55 mph towards midnight and 35-45 mph after sunrise the next morning as the gradient slowly weakened behind the front now well to the southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind was measured at Syracuse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm and frigid cold affected north central Kansas on New Year���s Day. Light to moderate snow developed during the early morning hours of New Year���s Day over western Kansas, then shifted into the local area between 5-6 AM CST. Temperatures were already cold in the teens and lower 20s, so accumulation began at the onset of snowfall. Snowfall rates peaked at near 1 to 2 inches per hour from after sunrise through about midday, before decreasing during the afternoon hours as dry air filtered in on breezy north winds. The combination of these gusty north winds of 30 to 35 MPH and dry, fluffy nature of the snow, blowing and drifting snow became a problem. Light snow continued through the afternoon hours before decreasing to mainly flurries during the evening. When all was said and done, snow amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches. Overall, this event generally under-achieved in terms of snowfall accumulations, likely due to the very cold and dry air mass filtering into the region. The continuation of elevated winds through the evening hours of New Year���s Day and into the early morning hours of January 2nd lead to very cold wind chills near -20F. Impacts from this event were not particularly severe as schools and many businesses were already closed due to the New Year���s holiday. However, snow covered roadways and poor visibility certainly produced a negative impact to regional general public travel, which was elevated due to the fact it was a holiday that landed on a weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts as high as 4 to 6 inches fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance from the west to southwest helped to produce heavy snow across much of the CWA. A mesoscale band in the mid levels stretched from east central Colorado into west central Kansas and towards south central Kansas where the most snow fell. 8-22 inches reported in this band, higher amounts in Scott County stretching to the northwest into the Goodland CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 inches was measured at a location 2W of Friend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status remained unchanged through October for Northwest Kansas.  The worst of the drought continued to be along and east of Highway 27 where a large area of D3 Extreme Drought was present.  Rawlins and Decatur counties continued in the D4 Exceptional Drought category.  Precipitation ranged from a hundredth of an inch to one and a half inches.  This was as much as two inches below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The precipitation for the month ranged from a hundredth of an inch to one inch.  This was half an inch to two inches below normal.  Normal precipitation ranges from one to two inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance from the west to southwest helped to produce heavy snow across much of the CWA. A mesoscale band in the mid levels stretched from east central Colorado into west central Kansas and towards south central Kansas where the most snow fell. 8-22 inches reported in this band, higher amounts in Scott County stretching to the northwest into the Goodland CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 10.5 inches was measured in Dodge City and Wright.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance in fast northwest upper level flow brought a narrow swath of light to moderate snow across the High Plains during the morning hours of January 5th, then shifted eastward into western portions of the forecast area around midday. A dry low level airmass limited snowfall coverage and intensity, initially, but persistent and focused lift allowed for accumulations to begin during the afternoon hours. Accumulating snow continued into the evening hours, mainly for areas along and southwest of a line from Smith Center to Beloit. Snow largely ended by midnight, but not before producing a swath of 2 to 6 inches, heaviest across Phillips County. This was a dry snow due to cold temperatures, but fortunately, wind speeds were light enough to preclude significant blowing and drifting snow. Impacts were fairly typical of winter weather advisory level events such as limited visibility during periods of moderate snow, along with snow covered roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts as high as 4 to 6 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits from the summer/early fall months continued into October, with severe drought (D2) continuing and even expanding into extreme drought (D3) for portions of far eastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the previous several months and continuing into October yielded severe (D2) to extreme (D3) across portions of far eastern Kansas. October began with northeastern Johnson County in D2 drought. By October 11th, the drought monitor showed D2 drought expanding to cover all of Johnson County with the northeastern quadrant of the county worsening to D3 drought through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits from the summer/early fall months continued into October, with severe drought (D2) continuing and even expanding into extreme drought (D3) for portions of far eastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the previous several months and continuing into October yielded severe (D2) to extreme (D3) across portions of far eastern Kansas. October began with the eastern half of Leavenworth County within D2 drought. By mid October D2 drought expanded to all over Leavenworth County, with this continuing through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the summer months and continuing into fall led to severe drought developing across portions of eastern Kansas by October 18th and continuing through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the summer months and continuing into fall led to severe drought developing across Doniphan County by October 18th and continuing through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large high precipitation supercell formed near the Nebraska Kansas border and tracked south during the evening of June 11th producing widespread damaging straight line winds of 80-110 mph along with several small tornadoes.   The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser showed video of what appeared to be a brief funnel near the ground in open country north of Interstate 70.   Any actual contact with the ground was brief and no damage was noted or reported.  The feature lost its organization as it moved south over I-70.

The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two surveys were done by NWS for this damage.   It appears that a very short path narrow tornado occurred embedded on the northeast flank of small enhanced downburst that created widespread damaging north to northwest winds.  Several homes were heavily damaged with the debris field blown to the northeast or approximately perpendicular to the movement.  It appears the tornado was narrow and only 25-50 yards at the widest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits from the summer/early fall months continued into October, with severe drought (D2) continuing and even expanding into extreme drought (D3) for portions of far eastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant precipitation deficits over the previous several months and continuing into October yielded severe (D2) to extreme (D3) across portions of far eastern Kansas. Linn County began October with the southern edge in D2 drought. D2 would quickly expand to cover the entire county, with even some areas of D3 drought across the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large high precipitation supercell formed near the Nebraska Kansas border and tracked south during the evening of June 11th producing widespread damaging straight line winds of 80-110 mph along with several small tornadoes.   The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed just east of Olsburg and tracked south southwest to the Spillway State Park on the southeast side Tuttle Creek Reservoir. The circulation became broad and dominated by the northwest winds of the RFD (rear flank downdraft) as it moved over the Tuttle Creek Dam and state park.  Enhanced destructive northwest winds then appeared to have descended into the city of Manhattan where widespread 80 to 100 mph winds were likely and did widespread damage.  Radar also indicated smaller microbursts where winds were likely in excess of 100 mph out of the NNW across the city of Manhattan.  NWS survey documented the worst damage in Manhattan where winds were likely 100 to 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the region as rainfall remained below seasonal normals.  Exceptional drought (D4) spread further north over most of southern Kansas during the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued across the county for the first part of the month, but by the middle of the month, Exceptional drought (D4) had spread across the entire county. Wichita Eisenhower National airport only recorded 0.79 inches of rainfall, which is 2.06 inches below normal. This is the fifth consecutive month of below normal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the region as rainfall remained below seasonal normals.  Exceptional drought (D4) spread further north over most of southern Kansas during the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) continued across the county, due to rainfall amounts well below normal.  Chanute airport only recorded 1.34 inches of rainfall, which is 1.99 inches below normal. This is the fifth consecutive month of below normal rainfall.

A strong low level jet from the southwest across the CWA prevailed ahead of cold front that tightened the pressure gradient significantly 9-10 mb from north to south. Wind gusts up to 60 to 70 mph were reported with a number of wildfires that sparked during the afternoon hours. Relative humidity values were down to 15-20 percent giving way to high wind and red flag warnings for the entire viewing area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire progressed rapidly just southeast of Garden City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to worsen across the region as rainfall remained below seasonal normals.  Exceptional drought (D4) spread further north over most of southern Kansas during the month of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought (D1) rapidly expanded to Extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month, due to well below normal rainfall across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across southeast Oklahoma into southeast Missouri during the early morning hours of February 17th.  Initial rain and thunderstorms impacted the area after midnight with surface temperatures below freezing.  This caused freezing rain and sleet accumulations of one quarter to one half inch in some spots.  The precipitation became all snow by sunrise with heavy snow and strong winds creating near blizzard conditions especially along a Emporia to Lawrence Kansas line and points south and east from there.  Many areas received .25 to .50 of ice with 2 to 10 of snow from this storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet and freezing rain with thunderstorms produced between one quarter and one half inch of sleet and freezing rain across much of the county.  The sleet turned to snow with anywhere from 6 to 10 inches of snow measured across the county.   In addition, north winds of 20 to 40 mph produced blowing and drifting of snow.  Douglas County Emergency management and NWS employees measured 6 to 10 inches of snow around the city of Lawrence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front dropped south across the forecast area early on February 1st.  As the colder air oozed into the area, increasing lift and moisture over the top of the shallow cold air led to a prolonged episode of moderate to heavy snow over southern Kansas during the daytime hours of February 2nd, 2022.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell across the county.  A Coop observer in Humboldt reported 4 inches.  A Coop observer 1 mile west of Iola reported 4 inches.  A Coop observer in Moran reported 3.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of the afternoon, winds shifted to the northwest across Northwest Kansas. Along with the wind shift, the winds also increased with gusts of 51-72 mph recorded via ASOS/AWOS stations and other mesonet stations for several counties along and north of I-70. The strongest winds were measured in Sherman and Rawlins counties. Dry soil conditions and the high winds produced localized areas of blowing dust across several counties with visibility ranging as low as 5 miles to near zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of dry soil conditions and high winds gusting up to 59 mph produced localized areas of blowing dust during the afternoon hours across the county. Visibility ranged from 7 miles to near zero at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought several rounds of wintry precipitation to southeast Kansas. Rain began falling late in the evening on February 1 and then changed to a period of sleet, freezing rain and then over to all snow during the early morning hours of February 2. There was a lull in the activity during the day February 2, though another round of snow moved in during the evening hours of February 2, with the snow continuing through the first half of the day February 3. Roads during this period became snow and ice covered and hazardous, with most schools closed both February 2 and February 3. Numerous accidents and slide offs were reported. A glaze of ice was reported in most locations with the initial round followed by 3 to 6 inches of storm total snow and sleet associated with the highest amounts along the Missouri border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were 2 rounds of wintry precipitation that fell across the county. Rain changed to a mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow during the early morning hours of February 2, then changed to all snow before ending around midday. There was a lull in the action before another round of just snow began late in the afternoon and continued into the early morning hours of February 3. The Cooperative observer in Pittsburg measured a total snow and sleet accumulation of 4.1 inches, along with a glaze of ice. Roads became ice and snow covered and hazardous, especially late in the day February 2 and continued February 3. Schools were closed across the county through the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough ejected out of the southwest U.S. and produced widespread light snow across the area.  There were a few bands of heavier snow across some western counties.  The snow had ended by midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 inches was reported on the north side of Dodge City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 14th, a cold front passed through northwestern Kansas during the afternoon. Winds behind this cold front gusted up to 60 mph causing blowing dust. Due to these winds, several automobile accidents were reported in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While most winds in the county stayed below high wind criteria, a pair of non-thunderstorm winds gusts up to 60 mph were reported by Mesonet stations in the county. The peak gust of 60 mph was reported 9 miles WSW of Winona, KS at 12:45 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 25th, an upper air disturbance moved over the High Plains region allowing for snow showers to develop over northwestern Kansas during the early morning that lasted into the evening hours. During this time, a snow band developed which caused heavy snowfall up to 27 inches in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall due to snow band was reported across Thomas county ranging between 1 and 6 inches. The heaviest snowfall of 6 inches was received via a delayed CoCoRaHS report at 4 miles east-northeast of Colby, KS. The portion of Interstate 70 that runs from the county border to Colby, KS was closed due to the snowfall. The criteria time based on radar imagery. Key impacts: heavy snow, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The D2 Severe Drought conditions continued for Northwest Kansas.  Precipitation amounts ranged from 0.25-2.00, with most of this coming from a historic heavy snow event on the 25th.  The heavy snow did cause Wallace and southern Sherman County to improve one drought category.  The monthly precipitation was either near or as much as two inches above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts ranged from 0.50-1.00 for the month.  Normal precipitation amounts range from 0.25-0.50.  The precipitation amounts were near normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on January 5th, a low pressure system moved over the Central High Plains bringing light to heavy snow accumulations across portions of Colorado, Nebraska, and Kansas through the pre-dawn hours of the 6th. In Northwest Kansas, snow accumulations varied from a trace to 4 inches along and south of I-70 with 2 to 8 inches of snow north of the interstate. The highest accumulations occurred in Norton and wester Decatur counties. At times, the snowfall rate and occasional gusts around 30 mph produced reduced visibility below 1 mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several bands of light to moderate snow moved generally south across Decatur County producing total snow accumulations ranging from 3 to 6 inches across the county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of the afternoon, winds shifted to the northwest across Northwest Kansas. Along with the wind shift, the winds also increased with gusts of 51-72 mph recorded via ASOS/AWOS stations and other mesonet stations for several counties along and north of I-70. The strongest winds were measured in Sherman and Rawlins counties. Dry soil conditions and the high winds produced localized areas of blowing dust across several counties with visibility ranging as low as 5 miles to near zero at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Halfway through February 2022, and for the first time since March 2021, the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM) assigned Severe Drought (D2) to a small portion of the six-county North Central Kansas area, specifically southern portions of Rooks and Osborne counties. During the 11-month interim during which D2 was held at bay, Moderate Drought (D1) made two appearances: 1) a fairly brief emergence into parts of Osborne/Mitchell counties during August 2021...2) a more recent re-introduction into mainly Rooks/Osborne counties in late-December 2021, followed by expansion to the entire six-county area in early-February (one week prior to the aforementioned introduction of D2). Digging a bit deeper into precipitation data, it is apparent that the February 2022 return of Severe Drought (D2) to southern Rooks/Osborne counties had been building for quite some time, given that the vast majority of the preceding nine months had featured prevailing below normal precipitation (a contributing factor to an outbreak of significant wildfires in/near the area in mid-December 2021). And as it turned out, this onset of D2 only signaled the start of a steadily-worsening drought situation within North Central Kansas through late winter/early spring 2022, as both precipitation and snowfall deficits continued to mount. ||Focusing specifically on the southern Rooks/Osborne counties area, and referencing data from the NWS cooperative observer in Natoma (one of the very-driest official weather stations in the region from mid-2021 into early-2022), the nine-month period from June 2021-Feb. 2022 was actually THE DRIEST on record out of 87 years with mostly-complete records! More specifically, Natoma measured merely 6.92 of precipitation through these nine months...an incredible 35% of normal. Interestingly, this long-lasting dry stretch kicked in right on the heels of a very wet May 2021, which featured 8.31 of rain (4th-wettest on record). Although it is relatively uncommon to see a two-category degradation in drought status during the winter months (in this case from D0 to D2), the extreme nature of the late 2021-early 2022 dryness proved to be an exception.

During the 11-month interim during which D2 was held at bay, Moderate Drought (D1) made two appearances: 1) a fairly brief emergence into parts of Osborne/Mitchell counties during August 2021...2) a more recent re-introduction into mainly Rooks/Osborne counties in late-December 2021, followed by expansion to the entire six-county area in early-February (one week prior to the aforementioned introduction of D2). Digging a bit deeper into precipitation data, it is apparent that the February 2022 return of Severe Drought (D2) to southern Rooks/Osborne counties had been building for quite some time, given that the vast majority of the preceding nine months had featured prevailing below normal precipitation (a contributing factor to an outbreak of significant wildfires in/near the area in mid-December 2021). And as it turned out, this onset of D2 only signaled the start of a steadily-worsening drought situation within North Central Kansas through late winter/early spring 2022, as both precipitation and snowfall deficits continued to mount. ||Focusing specifically on the southern Rooks/Osborne counties area, and referencing data from the NWS cooperative observer in Natoma (one of the very-driest official weather stations in the region from mid-2021 into early-2022), the nine-month period from June 2021-Feb. 2022 was actually THE DRIEST on record out of 87 years with mostly-complete records! More specifically, Natoma measured merely 6.92 of precipitation through these nine months...an incredible 35% of normal. Interestingly, this long-lasting dry stretch kicked in right on the heels of a very wet May 2021, which featured 8.31 of rain (4th-wettest on record). Although it is relatively uncommon to see a two-category degradation in drought status during the winter months (in this case from D0 to D2), the extreme nature of the late 2021-early 2022 dryness proved to be an exception.

A strong upper level disturbance from the west to southwest helped to produce heavy snow across much of the CWA. A mesoscale band in the mid levels stretched from east central Colorado into west central Kansas and towards south central Kansas where the most snow fell. 8-22 inches reported in this band, higher amounts in Scott County stretching to the northwest into the Goodland CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 inches was measured on the southwest side of Scott City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms moved out of the Oklahoma Panhandle to produce some 60mph wind gusts late in the evening towards midnight. Instability was weaker further north along with modest lapse rates and shear, but DCAPE values showed decent values to allow for outflows to push the severe thresholds into the CWA.

A low level jet of over 30 kts coupled with ample low level moisture with dewpoints into the 50s to near 60 helped to aid for MLCAPE values over 2000 J/kg. As a result, 1-2 inch hail reports occurred mostly in the isolated cells but a couple wind gust reports later in the evening when storms became more clustered before becoming linear further northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to be 60 MPH.  The airport had a gust to 61 MPH.

An extremely unstable airmass was in place across southern Kansas on May 31st as storms erupted along a stationary front from western Oklahoma into southern Kansas. Storms quickly became severe producing very large hail along with a couple brief tornadoes. In addition, they also produced very heavy rain that caused flash flooding along with subsequent river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was sent via Twitter of just shy of golf ball sized hail.

A wind event occurred mid to late evening across western portions of the CWA with radiational cooling after sunset. Being in the baroclinic zone of the storm system, high based convection occurred allowing for strong outflows with ample DCAPE values over 1200 J/kg. 60-70 mph was the frequent reports with only small hail occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to be 60 MPH.

On the morning of February 22nd, an arctic airmass moved into northwestern Kansas with breezy northerly winds following a cold front passage in the previous day. Wind chill values as low as -27 degrees Fahrenheit were recorded in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on wind chill values from surrounding counties, Rawlins county saw wind chill values as low as -26 degrees Fahrenheit within the area.  Impacts: extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry conditions and an unusually dry beginning to May, led to Severe (D2) drought worsening during the first part of May, to extreme (D3) drought. This was especially true across central Kansas. By the end of the month, welcome rainfall actually led to drought conditions improving by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued across the county, due to below normal rainfall during  the first half of the month.  Welcome rainfall towards the end of the month, improved the drought to severe (D2) by the end of the month.

A shortwave trough moved across the CWA with a dryline along the Hwy 283 corridor. The majority of the prime ingredients were to the east into south central Kansas and north central Oklahoma in the enhanced risk area. With decent shear and instability in the cold core part of the surface low, a few landspout tornadoes were produced along with some reports of severe hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms out of the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles collapsed with gust fronts extending into mainly southern counties across the CWA. A heat burst over these counties was the main cause of the high winds with a warning in place as up to 70 mph winds were reported. DCAPE values of over 1000 J/kg over the area with an already instilled gradient that allowed for such strong gusts. Another round of severe storms pushed out of the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles late evening into Morton County and moved northeast through sunset weakening with time. Shear profiles were weak, less than 30kts, within this marginal risk of western Kansas. Damaging winds and hail were the main threat with just a handful of reports near and around Elkhart.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of a tin roof on a small cabin was removed by high wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved slowly across northern Kansas and south central Kansas during the late afternoon of May 2nd, 2022.  A warm front associated with the low pressure area, lifted north into southern Kansas, with supercell thunderstorms developing in the warm sector, as the warm front lifted north.  The supercells produced some one inch hail reports and an isolated damaging wind report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry conditions and an unusually dry beginning to May, led to Severe (D2) drought worsening during the first part of May, to extreme (D3) drought. This was especially true across central Kansas. By the end of the month, welcome rainfall actually led to drought conditions improving by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) continued across the county, due to below normal rainfall during the month. Welcome rainfall towards the end of the month, improved conditions to a (D1) drought by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the morning of May 15, 2022 a complex of storms moved through the Kansas City metro, causing scattered wind damage. Most of the wind gusts were in the 60 to 70 mph range, and caused relatively minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 to 18 inch tree was down, blocking southbound lanes of US 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms out of the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles collapsed with gust fronts extending into mainly southern counties across the CWA. A heat burst over these counties was the main cause of the high winds with a warning in place as up to 70 mph winds were reported. DCAPE values of over 1000 J/kg over the area with an already instilled gradient that allowed for such strong gusts. Another round of severe storms pushed out of the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles late evening into Morton County and moved northeast through sunset weakening with time. Shear profiles were weak, less than 30kts, within this marginal risk of western Kansas. Damaging winds and hail were the main threat with just a handful of reports near and around Elkhart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Location was 4.4 NE of Hayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of February 22nd, an arctic airmass moved into northwestern Kansas with breezy northerly winds following a cold front passage in the previous day. Wind chill values as low as -27 degrees Fahrenheit were recorded in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning northerly sustained winds with speeds ranging between 22 to 27 mph combined with morning minimum temperatures as cold as -2.2 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -27 degrees Fahrenheit in Sherman County. The coldest wind chills were observed at the ASOS one mile north-northeast of Goodland, Kansas during the morning of February 22nd. Impacts: extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed ahead of a dryline along and east of Hwy 283 in an area of slight risk becoming linear and more of a damaging wind event. Small hail and winds up to 60mph were reported. Shear profiles were relatively weak less than 30 kts and with dewpoints in the 60s, ample CAPE values up to 1500 J/kg were evident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to be at least 60 MPH.

On the morning of February 22nd, an arctic airmass moved into northwestern Kansas with breezy northerly winds following a cold front passage in the previous day. Wind chill values as low as -27 degrees Fahrenheit were recorded in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning northerly sustained winds with speeds ranging between 25 to 27 mph combined with morning minimum temperatures as cold as -1 degree Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -26 degrees Fahrenheit in Decatur County. The coldest wind chills were observed at the AWOS station one mile north-northwest of Oberlin, Kansas during the morning of February 22nd. Impacts: extreme cold.

A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with the outflow of a thunderstorm moving across eastern Rawlins and western Decatur counties produced estimated 70 mph winds. Small tree limbs were reported down and siding torn off a building.

A strong pressure gradient was present across the CWA around 8-9 mb associated with a leeside trough deepened over eastern Colorado and ahead of an approaching cold front from the northwest. Winds gusted to as high as 50-60 mph with a widespread high wind warning in affect. Later in the day, along the dryline, severe activity developed coming out of Oklahoma and southeastern Colorado that saw hail and damaging winds as the main threat in an enhanced area for western counties. Ample deep layer shear and instability was present along the dryline as the leeside trough deepened throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Dewpoints in the 50s and 60s west to east and CAPE over 2000 to 2500 J/kg allowed for many hail and wind reports. Later in the evening, a line developed over eastern Colorado and progressed eastward through the overnight hours with damaging wind the main threat becoming non severe towards the Hwy 283 corridor losing the ingredients and less favorable area for severe reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a marginal severe risk, strong to severe storms developed in northeastern New Mexico and southeastern Colorado and pushed into southwest Kansas during the late afternoon spreading east northeast into the evening hours. An upper level trough deepened a low near this region providing the lift for these elevated storms. Modest low level moisture with dewpoints in the 50s with ample enough instability to produce some hail and damaging wind reports before weakening after sunset as the inversion set back up capping the environment that was eroded with daytime heating.

Severe thunderstorms developed and moved across much of central and northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening of April 29th.  The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and 5 confirmed tornadoes.    All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 or EF1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser video showed a small dust whirl under a funnel cloud.  The debris whirl didn't last long while the parent funnel continued on to the northeast.  Based upon the video of the debris near the ground it appears a weak tornado was on the ground for a short period in this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed and moved across much of central and northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening of April 29th.  The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and 5 confirmed tornadoes.    All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 or EF1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey revealed damage to structures including homes from a tornado that formed around or shortly before 8 pm CDT just to the southeast of Hope, KS.  The tornado tracked to the ENE passing approximately 2 miles north of Herington KS before moving into Morris county KS.  EF1 damage was noted at 2 properties including garage damage to one home.

Severe thunderstorms developed and moved across much of central and northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening of April 29th.  The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and 5 confirmed tornadoes.    All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 or EF1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser took photos of a tornado that developed just west of K15 highway shortly before 8pm CDT.  The tornado moved ESE over open country as it moved out of Dickinson county and into Marion county Kansas.  No damage was reported with this tornado in Dickinson county although some damage was documented in northern Marion county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey revealed the tornado tracked into Morris county and damaged several farms and homes along its path to Parkerville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar and NWS storm survey revealed that as the tornado moved back into southern Pottawatomie county that it widened and produced EF1 damage to several irrigation pivots, an outbuilding and finally took part of a roof off a home in Belvue before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed and moved across much of central and northeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening of April 29th.  The storms produced large hail, damaging winds and 5 confirmed tornadoes.    All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 or EF1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm survey revealed a narrow damage path from what appears to have been a small tornado that developed over the Prairie Band of Potawatomi Nation Reservation.  The tornado destroyed a large Morton style outbuilding and produced minor shingle damage to several homes and a community center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 59 mph were recorded within Greeley county. Three Mesonet stations within 10 miles of Tribune, KS recorded the maximum wind gust speed of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on April 29 elevated thunderstorms moved eastward just south of Interstate 70. These storms were marginally severe, and with a brief exception only provided enough organization for isolated golfball sized hail. Later in the evening, widespread severe thunderstorms formed in central and eastern Nebraska and Kansas. As these storms approached the Kansas, Nebraska, Missouri border area they formed a potent line of storms that caused some 60 to 70 mph wind and some minor damage across far eastern Kansas and western Missouri during the overnight hours of April 29, going into early April 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening of April 23, 2022, strong thunderstorms moved into northwest Missouri and northeast Kansas. These storms were primarily wind makers, with scattered 60+ mph reports and some minor damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree around 12 inches in diameter was uprooted near Highland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow with a tight gradient roughly about 10mb across the high plains of western Kansas. Gusty northwest winds developed late morning lasting through the peak heating of the afternoon seeing 50-60 mph for most of the CWA. Winds subsided after sunset below the warning threshold but ramped up once again with daytime heating mixing boundary layer winds to the surface by late morning through the afternoon continuing to gust up to 50-60 mph before diminishing after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed on Highway 50 near mile marker 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported via KCBK at Colby Shaltz Field.

Damage from these winds included a semi-truck blown over near mile marker 13 on I-70 1 mile southeast of Caruso; a trailer blown over at mile marker 3 on I-70 1 mile southeast of Kanorado; a blown over carport, a snapped off 4-inch diameter tree branch, a blown over shed, and a damaged roof panel in Goodland; and a downed 10 ft. tall live tree of 6-8 inches in diameter at 13 miles north of Edson.

A portion of Kansas Highway 96 near Marienthal, KS was closed during the fire due to smoke reducing visibility greatly. Rainfall from storms in the area helped in the firefighting efforts to put out the fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of at least 65 MPH blew an RV over 4 miles ENE of Bloom on Highway 54.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at a Mesonet station locations 5 miles south of Selden, KS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours, a low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains bringing high winds and thunderstorms to Northwest Kansas. With the thunderstorms, pea to quarter sized hail was reported with winds gusting up to 67 mph reported. Due to the high winds and drought conditions over the area, blowing dust occurred across several counties with visibility ranging from 7 miles to one-quarter of a mile or less. The lowest reductions in visibility occurred in Norton and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced between 4 and 6 inches of rainfall leading to flooding across southeastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets around Columbus along with several other rural roads around the county were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms affected the region during the overnight into the morning hours of May 15th, 2022.  Most of the severe events were around one inch, however a few hail stone reports up to 1.5 inches in diameter were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail size varied between pea to quarter sized hail.  This report was relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds knocked a grain bin trailer onto another farm vehicle and also ripped off portions of a metal barn wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.

The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds caused a grain bin on the north side of a property to be dented near the top and pushed out the bottom third of a metal building's wall on the south side of the property. There were also some tree branches of an unknown size blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upon further review and information/video gathered, it was determined that a tornado moved across extreme southern Sheridan County. This tornado was confirmed by damage from NWS Storm Survey, tornado chaser video, and a brief/weak tornadic debris signature on NWS Goodland WSR 88D radar imagery. Damage consisted of several large healthy tree limbs snapped, one treat snapped near the base, and a power pole down. Based on the damage, maximum wind speed was determined to be 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours, a low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains bringing high winds and thunderstorms to Northwest Kansas. With the thunderstorms, pea to quarter sized hail was reported with winds gusting up to 67 mph reported. Due to the high winds and drought conditions over the area, blowing dust occurred across several counties with visibility ranging from 7 miles to one-quarter of a mile or less. The lowest reductions in visibility occurred in Norton and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds gusting up to 55 mph out of the north-northwest according to the Norton AWOS station combined with the drought conditions produced blowing dust with visibility ranging from 7 miles to one-quarter mile near the city of Norton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning to midday hours, a line of storms developed along the dryline in Southwest Kansas, expanding north into Northwest Kansas. As the line moved across Norton and Graham counties, the storms produced up to half dollar sized hail and wind gusts up to 62 mph. Along with the storms, there was a wall of blowing dust that originated in extreme Southwest Nebraska and tracked southeast into portions of Northwest Kansas during the late afternoon. Visibility reductions as low as one-quarter mile were observed, with the greatest reductions in Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across Thomas County, a plume of dust moved southeast producing minimum visibilities as low as two miles at times. Maximum gust of 45 mph was recorded by KCBK AWOS in Colby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid to late afternoon hours, scattered storms developed initially over Greeley and Wichita counties, then continued to form to the northeast across Northwest Kansas into Norton County. These storms produced hail up to quarters in size across portions of Greeley, Logan, and Thomas counties. As the storms began to decay, outflow produced high wind gusts up to 95 mph with damage in and near the town of Norcatur, KS in Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny sized hail observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant wildfire began in Decatur County, Kansas and moved into Furnas and Red Willow Counties in Nebraska. The fire was confined to the far northeastern part of the county as it started here and quickly burned north-northeast. A multi-mission aircraft from Colorado mapped the fire and showed it to have burned an area 28 miles long across 44,024 total acres, putting the number of acres burned in Decatur County around 1,000 acres, with Red Willow County at around 28,000 and 15,000 in Furnas County, NE. Resources from all over were deployed to help with this fire, including 3 Nebraska Air National Guard Blackhawk Helicopters and a Type 1 National Incident Management Team. Total personnel exceeded 100 at times. In addition, a NWS Incident Meteorologist (IMET) was deployed to this fire, the first deployment of an NWS IMET in our forecast area's history. Fighting the fire was difficult due to the rural/terrain area it was in, as well as persistent blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon storms that lasted into the night, caused by an upper-level disturbance, produced an orphaned outflow boundary (an outflow boundary that is not located near the storm that produced it). The orphaned outflow caused a wind gust of 61 MPH. The storms that created the outflow formed in Colorado and then traveled northeast into western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station at Hill City airport. Peak southerly gust was at 61 MPH. Strong winds were associated with the passage of an orphaned outflow boundary (Outflow that persisted well after storms have dissipated).

The storms began forming in Colorado and traveled northeast through Northwest Kansas and Southwest Nebraska.  Meanwhile high winds occurred behind a boundary that was behind a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report from MPING of dime sized hail (.75 IN).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked across mainly open country causing some damage to outbuildings and trees along its path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station measured a peak gust of 59 mph east of the town of Lincoln and west of Beverly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across the Northern and Central Plains producing strong to high winds across Northwest Kansas during the morning hours. A large portion of the area had peak winds between 50 and 57 mph; however, the city of Atwood had the strongest winds with a peak gust of 59 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed via mesonet station GW1727 in Atwood, Kansas. Winds were out of the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved south across extreme Southwest Nebraska and Northwest Kansas during the afternoon hours on the 17th. As storms developed along the front, brief landspout tornadoes were reported in northern Decatur and Gove counties. These stayed mainly over fields, producing no damage. There were also reports of pea to quarter sized hail with the line of storms. The largest hail report was also located in Decatur County in the town of Jennings. During the evening, a line of storms moved east across the Colorado border eventually connecting with the earlier line of storms impacting areas along I-70 as far east as Beloit, Kansas. When the storms moved over Sherman County it produced up to 58 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of landspout tornadoes along the Kansas and Nebraska border. Based on the reports received, estimated locations of the reports, and an interpretation of WSR-88D radar imagery, it was determined that the reports were the result of one single landspout tornado in northeastern Decatur County. The tornado remained over open fields and no damage was report.

This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported via Weather Underground station kkscolby35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 5th, a cold front passed through northwestern Kansas allowing for strong northerly winds to occur in the area. While most gusts stayed below severe weather criteria, a high wind gust of 58 mph was recorded in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was recorded by the KGLD ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved south across extreme Southwest Nebraska and Northwest Kansas during the afternoon hours on the 17th. As storms developed along the front, brief landspout tornadoes were reported in northern Decatur and Gove counties. These stayed mainly over fields, producing no damage. There were also reports of pea to quarter sized hail with the line of storms. The largest hail report was also located in Decatur County in the town of Jennings. During the evening, a line of storms moved east across the Colorado border eventually connecting with the earlier line of storms impacting areas along I-70 as far east as Beloit, Kansas. When the storms moved over Sherman County it produced up to 58 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighter reported landspout tornado just north of the town of Park. Time and exact locations estimated from radar, putting in extreme northern Gove County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms tracked across south central Kansas in the early morning hours of April 29th. These storms produced large hail up to two inches in diameter near Andale.

Storms that developed in western Kansas moved into central Kansas during the evening. These storms diminished as they tracked east, but one severe storm did occur in Barton County which had golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barton County dispatch relayed a public report of golf ball sized hail near Pawnee Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds had sustained speeds up to 58 mph with a peak gust of 79 mph. The visibility was reduced due to blowing dust down to one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed little from the previous day of high winds and blowing dust. The upper level disturbance continued to slide northeast over the Upper Midwest. High winds rounding the backside of the disturbance were able to reach the ground over Northwest Kansas. The peak gust was 70 MPH at the Goodland airport. Dust storms also occurred due to the high winds and dry ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were no wind gusts of 58 MPH or greater reported in the county. However surrounding counties did report winds of 58 MPH or greater.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damaging quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) tornado moved across Sharon Springs, Kansas during the late evening/overnight hours of April 22nd, 2022. Maximum winds were found to be around 110 mph, which makes this tornado an EF-1, just short of an EF-2. The worst damage was noted at the KDOT station near Highway 40 where a cinder block building was toppled and a truck shed was completely destroyed, as well as the CHS facility where the office building had its roof completely torn off. Throughout town, there were many tree limbs broken, tree trunks broken, and trees uprooted. Many buildings and vehicles had broken windows. Power poles were broken or downed throughout town. Other damage included several rolled trailers (ag-related, RVs, or work trailers), fences blown down, shingles off roofs, and other wind-related damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 58 MPH were reported across the county. The highest gust reported was at the Colby airport.  A camper was blown over on I-70 three miles east of Brewster, and a semi was blowing over two miles east of Brewster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day a strong upper level disturbance centered over the upper Midwest caused wind gusts up to 71 MPH to occur over Northwest Kansas. The highest wind gust occurred in Rawlins County. The high winds over dry ground caused localized dust storms to occur at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 61 MPH occurred across the county.  The highest wind gusts were measured northwest of St. Francis.
