EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon to early evening hours, a disturbance moved over the High Plains creating isolated to scattered thunderstorms that produced multiple tornadoes, up to ping pong ball sized hail, and damaging winds with gusts up to 90 mph.  The wind damage occurred in Decatur, Sheridan and Graham counties, with damage to an airport hanger in the city of Hoxie, damage to an outbuilding and utility trailer, and numerous large tree limbs downed. The largest hail was reported in southern Logan County while the strongest winds were reported in Hoxie with the damage to the airplane hanger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a thunderstorm moved into Hoxie, winds estimated to gust up to 90 mph damaged an airplane hangar at the Hoxie Municipal Airport. Damage included the loss of roof and damage to the side walls. The wood used as supporting structure on the hangar was in fair condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved over Northwest. A corridor of high winds from the upper level disturbance reached the ground over Sherman, Cheyenne, and Rawlins counties. The highest reported wind gust was 61 MPH in Rawlins County. The high winds led to dust storm conditions north of Atwood and outside of Goodland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust reduced the visibility down to a quarter mile just outside of Goodland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning to midday hours, a line of storms developed along the dryline in Southwest Kansas, expanding north into Northwest Kansas. As the line moved across Norton and Graham counties, the storms produced up to half dollar sized hail and wind gusts up to 62 mph. Along with the storms, there was a wall of blowing dust that originated in extreme Southwest Nebraska and tracked southeast into portions of Northwest Kansas during the late afternoon. Visibility reductions as low as one-quarter mile were observed, with the greatest reductions in Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across Rawlins County, a plume of dust moved southeast producing minimum visibilities as low as two miles at times. Wind gusts up to 53 mph were reported at the KADT AWOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported most of the hail was pea to nickel size, but a few stones were around two inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month the drought status worsened for most of Northwest Kansas.  By the end of the month Rawlins and Decatur counties had fallen to D3 Extreme Drought.  Otherwise the only county not in D2 Severe Drought was Wallace.  Up to four inches of precipitation occurred, with the highest amounts in Norton County.  This was a departure of a half inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of one to three inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning to midday hours, a line of storms developed along the dryline in Southwest Kansas, expanding north into Northwest Kansas. As the line moved across Norton and Graham counties, the storms produced up to half dollar sized hail and wind gusts up to 62 mph. Along with the storms, there was a wall of blowing dust that originated in extreme Southwest Nebraska and tracked southeast into portions of Northwest Kansas during the late afternoon. Visibility reductions as low as one-quarter mile were observed, with the greatest reductions in Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across Logan County a plume of dust moved southeast, clipping the northwest corner of the county and producing minimum visibilities as low as four miles. KOEL AWOS reported a maximum gust of 39 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid to late afternoon hours, scattered storms developed initially over Greeley and Wichita counties, then continued to form to the northeast across Northwest Kansas into Norton County. These storms produced hail up to quarters in size across portions of Greeley, Logan, and Thomas counties. As the storms began to decay, outflow produced high wind gusts up to 95 mph with damage in and near the town of Norcatur, KS in Decatur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32 foot south facing cattle shed was torn off its anchors and rolled several yards across the property. The strong winds were produced along the outflow of a decaying thunderstorm moving over the Norcatur, KS area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.

An extremely unstable airmass was in place across southern Kansas on May 31st as storms erupted along a stationary front from western Oklahoma into southern Kansas. Storms quickly became severe producing very large hail along with a couple brief tornadoes. In addition, they also produced very heavy rain that caused flash flooding along with subsequent river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a rainfall total for tonight thus far at 21st and Central.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant weather system impacted the High Plains throughout the day on April 22nd and into the early morning hours of April 23rd. This weather system was not only a prolific wind producer, which led to many reports of high wind and wind damage, but also dust storm conditions and several large fires to occur.  The largest fire occurred in northeast Decatur County, burning north into Nebraska.  This fire was not extinguished until the 29th. During the afternoon and early evening hours of the 22nd, storms began to form along a dryline across northwestern Kansas and southwestern Nebraska. These storms primarily produced large hail up to baseball size, with a few instances of damaging thunderstorm winds. Later in the evening, a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) developed over eastern Colorado, spreading east across northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the overnight hours. These storms produced several tornadoes, along with thunderstorm wind gusts and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds snapped a living tree roughly 6 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as a band of moderate snowfall that moved over Greeley County over a span of several hours. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 5.5 to 8 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed in Tribune, Kansas.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought cold rain, wet snow, and strong winds to north central Kansas during the early morning and daytime hours of March 22nd. A potent, neutral to negatively tilted upper trough tracked south of the local area, generally from southern Kansas and northern Oklahoma into Missouri. This placed north central Kansas within the northern semi-circle of the storm system and under a broad swath of deformation precipitation, which was much needed after a fairly dry winter. For the most part, temperatures remained just warm enough to keep most of the precipitation as a rain and snow mix, or just a cold rain. However, between 2AM CST and 11AM CST, rain was able to change over to mostly wet snow across far southern portions of Rooks and Osborne counties. In fact, a Cooperative Observer a few miles west-northwest of Plainville recorded a snow amount of 6.7 inches. Other observations around the Plainville area were generally 3 to 6 inches. Most of Osborne County likely only received a trace to 1 inch of snow, but the far southwest corner of the county received 1 to 3 inches. Another aspect of the strong storm system was high wind gusts. Wind gusts over 45 MPH were common, especially during the daytime hours of the 22nd, and there was even one measured wind gust as high as 58 MPH in Jewell County. As a result of the combination of strong wind, wet snow, and ice, a local utility company stated that the Osborne service area sustained significant damage to power lines and power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 MPH was recorded by a mesonet station located in Randall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed across the High Plains in the very early morning hours of March 21st. As the low progressed slowly east then northeast, a significant band of heavy snowfall developed across eastern Colorado. Additional snowfall developed over northwestern Kansas but was generally light in nature. However, the heavy snowband that formed over eastern Colorado did spread into northwestern Sherman County Kansas as it was weakening, which did lead to some higher snowfall amounts across a small portion of Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration light snowfall event was experienced across Sherman County. In addition, a heavy snowband that formed earlier in the day across eastern Colorado moved into the extreme northwest portions of the county. Snowfall across Sherman County ranged from around 2 inches in the east to 6 inches reported north of Ruleton, or the northwest corner of the county. In addition, Interstate 70 was closed from Limon to Goodland as a result of the extreme snow amounts reported over eastern Colorado, but roads across Sherman County were also reported to be snowpacked. Key impacts: light to moderate snow, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Wednesday was only an opening act to many markedly windy days within North Central Kansas during April 2022 (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports this was likely the overall-windiest April since at least 1996). Primarily between the mid-morning and late afternoon hours, sustained northwesterly winds were commonly at least 25-35 MPH with frequent gusts 45-55 MPH...and there were a few measured marginally-severe criteria gusts up to 58 MPH. The overall-strongest winds targeted Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties, including unofficial peak gusts of 58 MPH at Phillipsburg airport (AWOS) and Logan (mesonet). Although there were no known reports of notable wind damage, the combination of strong winds and ongoing drought conditions promoted fairly widespread areas of blowing dust, reducing visibility to one mile or less in various locations. ||In the mid-upper levels, the main player in what would end up being a two-day high wind event for the local area (along with the 7th) was a powerful/deep closed low churning over the northern Minnesota/Wisconsin region. At the surface, a nearly co-located intense low pressure center bottomed out around 988 millibars across MN/WI during the day. This placed the state of Kansas in a favorable environment for high winds within the southwest quadrant of the large-scale low. At mid-day (18Z), a respectable northeast-to-southwest pressure gradient of 12 millibars was noted across the state (ranging from 1006 millibars in far northeast KS to 1018 millibars far southwest). Furthermore, steep low-level lapse rates allowed efficient downward momentum transfer from aloft during the daylight hours, with wind speeds in the 850-700 millibar layer averaging 40-50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with periods of sustained northwesterly speeds near 40 MPH, stronger gusts also occurred. The peak measured gust by the Phillipsburg Airport AWOS was 58 MPH, at 3:15 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day a strong upper level disturbance centered over the upper Midwest caused wind gusts up to 71 MPH to occur over Northwest Kansas. The highest wind gust occurred in Rawlins County. The high winds over dry ground caused localized dust storms to occur at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were no wind gusts of 58 MPH or greater reported in the county.  However surrounding counties did report winds of 58 MPH or greater.  The visibility was reduced down to five miles for a brief time at Tribune due to blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day a strong upper level disturbance centered over the upper Midwest caused wind gusts up to 71 MPH to occur over Northwest Kansas. The highest wind gust occurred in Rawlins County. The high winds over dry ground caused localized dust storms to occur at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 63 MPH occurred across the county.  The highest wind gust reported was at the Oakley airport.  Blowing dust reduced the visibility down to four miles for a brief time at Oakley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought extreme fire weather conditions to north central Kansas  during the afternoon and evening hours of March 29th. An upper level trough deepened nearly directly over the local area during the day, which resulted in a tightening pressure gradient at the surface. Hot, dry air and resultant extreme fire conditions were pulled up from the southwest and into northern Kansas. Several wildfires were reported in the region on this day, but by far the most notable fire locally occurred in Jewell County. The fire started in the early afternoon just northwest of Burr Oak, then quickly raced north-northeast owing to intense wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH. The northern edge of the fire reached the Kansas, Nebraska state line by the evening, before a strong cold front moved in and switched winds around to the northwest. This wind shift resulted in several flare-ups through the 30th. No homes were lost to the fire, but a few outbuildings and fencing were burned along with approximately 12,000 acres of land. Another impact was significant smoke that was blown northward, which resulted in the closure of Highway 78 and Highway 136. A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was recorded by a mesonet just west-southwest of Beloit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was recorded by a mesonet station located four miles west-southwest of Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-upper level trough axis made its way through the Rockies on Friday the 22nd, with a more organized circulation deepening Friday night into early Saturday morning the 23rd as it tracked into the central and northern High Plains. Through the rest of the day on the 23rd this system continued to deepen and become increasingly vertically-stacked, gradually sliding northeast into the central South Dakota/North Dakota border area by that evening. In response to this approaching system, surface low pressure intensified over northeastern Colorado into the southern border of Nebraska/Wyoming on the 22nd, bottoming out around 984 millibars by early evening. With North Central Kansas sandwiched between this surface low and high pressure ridging southward through the entire eastern CONUS, the result was a tightening pressure gradient and intense southerly winds. Sustained southerly speeds of 35-45 MPH and gusts of 45-55 MPH were common across the area by midday on the 22nd.||From early evening and on through the overnight hours, periodic gusts of 55-65 MPH occurred locally, with a peak gust of 65 MPH measured just after 11 p.m. CDT by a mesonet station eight miles north of Bellaire. During the mid-late evening hours, an organized line of thunderstorms developed along the surface cold front over western Nebraska and Kansas, as the main mid-upper level system pushed out of the Rockies into western Nebraska and South Dakota. This activity tracked east through North Central Kansas mainly between 2-6 a.m. CDT on the 23rd. While most of the storms were sub-severe, increasing deep-layer shear and downdraft CAPE values near 1000 J/kg aided in a handful of storms producing severe gusts, including a peak gust of 67 MPH clocked by a mesonet station near Stockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 67 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located one mile south-southwest of Stockton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Wednesday was only an opening act to many markedly windy days within North Central Kansas during April 2022 (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports this was likely the overall-windiest April since at least 1996). Primarily between the mid-morning and late afternoon hours, sustained northwesterly winds were commonly at least 25-35 MPH with frequent gusts 45-55 MPH...and there were a few measured marginally-severe criteria gusts up to 58 MPH. The overall-strongest winds targeted Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties, including unofficial peak gusts of 58 MPH at Phillipsburg airport (AWOS) and Logan (mesonet). Although there were no known reports of notable wind damage, the combination of strong winds and ongoing drought conditions promoted fairly widespread areas of blowing dust, reducing visibility to one mile or less in various locations. ||In the mid-upper levels, the main player in what would end up being a two-day high wind event for the local area (along with the 7th) was a powerful/deep closed low churning over the northern Minnesota/Wisconsin region. At the surface, a nearly co-located intense low pressure center bottomed out around 988 millibars across MN/WI during the day. This placed the state of Kansas in a favorable environment for high winds within the southwest quadrant of the large-scale low. At mid-day (18Z), a respectable northeast-to-southwest pressure gradient of 12 millibars was noted across the state (ranging from 1006 millibars in far northeast KS to 1018 millibars far southwest). Furthermore, steep low-level lapse rates allowed efficient downward momentum transfer from aloft during the daylight hours, with wind speeds in the 850-700 millibar layer averaging 40-50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with periods of sustained northwesterly speeds near 40 MPH, stronger gusts of 50-60 MPH also likely occurred, based on surrounding area observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought extreme fire weather conditions to north central Kansas  during the afternoon and evening hours of March 29th. An upper level trough deepened nearly directly over the local area during the day, which resulted in a tightening pressure gradient at the surface. Hot, dry air and resultant extreme fire conditions were pulled up from the southwest and into northern Kansas. Several wildfires were reported in the region on this day, but by far the most notable fire locally occurred in Jewell County. The fire started in the early afternoon just northwest of Burr Oak, then quickly raced north-northeast owing to intense wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH. The northern edge of the fire reached the Kansas, Nebraska state line by the evening, before a strong cold front moved in and switched winds around to the northwest. This wind shift resulted in several flare-ups through the 30th. No homes were lost to the fire, but a few outbuildings and fencing were burned along with approximately 12,000 acres of land. Another impact was significant smoke that was blown northward, which resulted in the closure of Highway 78 and Highway 136. A peak wind gust of 59 MPH was recorded by a mesonet just west-southwest of Beloit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This wildfire started in the early afternoon and was ignited along 90 Road just south of X Road, approximately two and one-half miles northwest of Burr Oak. Driven by south-southwesterly winds gusting near 60 MPH at times, this fire quickly spread north-northeast. This fire remained east of 90 Road and west of 140 Road, with its northern edge right along the Kansas-Nebraska state line. Crews worked to get the fire under control into the early morning hours of the 30th. A few minor flare-ups occurred during the day on the 30th, driven in part by a switch in winds to the north-northwest, gusting over 45 MPH at times. In total, approximately 12,000 acres were burned. While no homes were lost, a few old outbuildings were burned, as well as fencing. There was minor damage to one fire department's tanker truck due to an accident. The large nature of the fire and strong winds blew smoke well to the north, resulting in low visibility and the closure of Highway 128 north of Burr Oak, Highway 78 south of Guide Rock in Webster County NE, and Highway 136 on either side of the Webster County and Nuckolls County NE border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month the drought status worsened for most of Northwest Kansas.  By the end of the month Rawlins and Decatur counties had fallen to D3 Extreme Drought.  Otherwise the only county not in D2 Severe Drought was Wallace.  Up to four inches of precipitation occurred, with the highest amounts in Norton County.  This was a departure of a half inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of one to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts for the county ranged from 0.25-2.00.  This was half an inch to two inches below the normal precipitation amounts of 2.00-3.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swath of heavy snow occurred over portions of north central Kansas on March 9th and 10th. Snow developed just after sunrise on March 9th, initially along the I-80 corridor in south central Nebraska, then slowly shifted southward throughout the day, arriving along the state line during the mid to late afternoon hours. Air temperatures were well below normal for early March, which allowed snow to readily accumulate from the onset and begin to cause slick roads by the evening of the 9th. Snow intensity increased during the evening hours, especially for areas along the Highway 36 corridor where a slow moving band of moderate to heavy snow developed and persisted overnight. Snow intensities only gradually decreased during the morning hours of the 10th. Despite normal high temperatures in the 40s, actual air temperatures remained cold throughout the event in the 20s, which is actually near or below normal LOW temperatures. This led to a primarily dry and fluffy type of snowfall. Total snowfall accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common for Phillips, Smith, and Jewell counties. However, much greater amounts occurred in Rooks, Osborne, and Mitchell counties where 7 to 13 inches of snow fell. For these areas, this would end up as the biggest single event snowfall of the season. Impacts typical of an event of this magnitude, such as slick roads and closures of local schools and businesses, occurred on the 9th and 10th.|This snowfall was forced primarily by persistently moderate to strong frontogenesis in the 700mb to 500mb layer amidst fast, zonal upper flow and a weak upper level disturbance. Moisture availability was pretty limited with this system, however, the aforementioned cold temperatures led to a relatively efficient snowfall, with both duration and high snow to liquid ratios contributing to the heavy snow amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts were estimated to be between 4 to 8 inches across the county. A total of 6.0 inches was recorded one mile west of Logan, with 5.8 inches recorded six miles east of Phillipsburg.

This was a departure of a half inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of one to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts for the county ranged from 0.01-1.00.  This was an inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of 1.50-3.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours, a low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains bringing high winds and thunderstorms to Northwest Kansas. With the thunderstorms, pea to quarter sized hail was reported with winds gusting up to 67 mph reported. Due to the high winds and drought conditions over the area, blowing dust occurred across several counties with visibility ranging from 7 miles to one-quarter of a mile or less. The lowest reductions in visibility occurred in Norton and Sheridan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail reported, with most hail stones reported to be the size of dimes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 19th, a cold front passed through the northwestern region of Kansas. Due to this cold front, winds gusted up to around 62 mph causing blowing dust that lowered visibility down to near zero miles within portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 54 mph were reported within Sherman County. The KGLD ASOS located 1 mile north-northeast of Goodland, KS recorded the maximum non-thunderstorm wind gust of 54 mph at its location. These winds caused blowing dust that lowered visibility down to between 2 miles in the area. Power outages were also reported 3 miles west of Goodland, KS due to these winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich gulf moisture streamed north of a slow moving low pressure for the afternoon and overnight hours of May 4th into the early morning of May 5th, 2022.  Heavy rainfall impacted a large portions of southern Kansas, with some flooding reported.  Rainfall amounts of 4 to 5 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station in Eureka, Kansas, reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 2.60 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as two broad bands of moderate snowfall that moved over Sherman County. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 4.8 to 7 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed in Goodland, Kansas. A few snow reports from the evening hours on the 9th also mention localized blowing snow with visibilities reduced to around one mile due to northeast winds gusting up to 25 mph and the moderate snowfall rate.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-upper level trough axis made its way through the Rockies on Friday the 22nd, with a more organized circulation deepening Friday night into early Saturday morning the 23rd as it tracked into the central and northern High Plains. Through the rest of the day on the 23rd this system continued to deepen and become increasingly vertically-stacked, gradually sliding northeast into the central South Dakota/North Dakota border area by that evening. In response to this approaching system, surface low pressure intensified over northeastern Colorado into the southern border of Nebraska/Wyoming on the 22nd, bottoming out around 984 millibars by early evening. With North Central Kansas sandwiched between this surface low and high pressure ridging southward through the entire eastern CONUS, the result was a tightening pressure gradient and intense southerly winds. Sustained southerly speeds of 35-45 MPH and gusts of 45-55 MPH were common across the area by midday on the 22nd.||From early evening and on through the overnight hours, periodic gusts of 55-65 MPH occurred locally, with a peak gust of 65 MPH measured just after 11 p.m. CDT by a mesonet station eight miles north of Bellaire. During the mid-late evening hours, an organized line of thunderstorms developed along the surface cold front over western Nebraska and Kansas, as the main mid-upper level system pushed out of the Rockies into western Nebraska and South Dakota. This activity tracked east through North Central Kansas mainly between 2-6 a.m. CDT on the 23rd. While most of the storms were sub-severe, increasing deep-layer shear and downdraft CAPE values near 1000 J/kg aided in a handful of storms producing severe gusts, including a peak gust of 67 MPH clocked by a mesonet station near Stockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained southerly speeds near or exceeding 40 MPH were common during the day, along with gusts near 60 MPH at times. A peak wind gust of 65 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located eight miles north of Bellaire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as two bands of moderate snowfall that moved over Cheyenne County. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 3.5 to 7 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed south of Wheeler, Kansas along the Cheyenne and Sherman county line. A few snow reports from the evening hours on the 9th also mention localized blowing snow due to northeast winds gusting up to 25 mph and the moderate snowfall rate.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swath of heavy snow occurred over portions of north central Kansas on March 9th and 10th. Snow developed just after sunrise on March 9th, initially along the I-80 corridor in south central Nebraska, then slowly shifted southward throughout the day, arriving along the state line during the mid to late afternoon hours. Air temperatures were well below normal for early March, which allowed snow to readily accumulate from the onset and begin to cause slick roads by the evening of the 9th. Snow intensity increased during the evening hours, especially for areas along the Highway 36 corridor where a slow moving band of moderate to heavy snow developed and persisted overnight. Snow intensities only gradually decreased during the morning hours of the 10th. Despite normal high temperatures in the 40s, actual air temperatures remained cold throughout the event in the 20s, which is actually near or below normal LOW temperatures. This led to a primarily dry and fluffy type of snowfall. Total snowfall accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common for Phillips, Smith, and Jewell counties. However, much greater amounts occurred in Rooks, Osborne, and Mitchell counties where 7 to 13 inches of snow fell. For these areas, this would end up as the biggest single event snowfall of the season. Impacts typical of an event of this magnitude, such as slick roads and closures of local schools and businesses, occurred on the 9th and 10th.|This snowfall was forced primarily by persistently moderate to strong frontogenesis in the 700mb to 500mb layer amidst fast, zonal upper flow and a weak upper level disturbance. Moisture availability was pretty limited with this system, however, the aforementioned cold temperatures led to a relatively efficient snowfall, with both duration and high snow to liquid ratios contributing to the heavy snow amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts ranging from 7 to 13 inches were reported across the county. Some of the highest totals included 12.8 inches from one mile east of Codell, 12.7 inches from four miles west-northwest of Plainville, and 10 inches from seven miles northeast of Plainville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Thursday marked the second straight day of intense north-northwest winds across North Central Kansas, with most of the daylight hours featuring relentless sustained speeds commonly 30-40 MPH and frequent gusts 45-55 MPH (with sporadic, marginally-severe higher gusts). Per various mesonet stations, peak gusts featured: 60 MPH in Jewell, 58 MPH a few miles west-northwest of Jewell (KSU sensor) and 56 MPH at Smith Center airport. Like the preceding day, there were no known reports of notable wind damage, but the combination of relentless wind and worsening drought conditions promoted fairly widespread areas of blowing dust, reducing visibility to one mile or less in various spots. Sustained speeds finally eased down below 30 MPH (and gusts below 45 MPH) on a consistent basis by around 9 p.m. CDT on the 7th, signaling the end of back-to-back days of higher-end winds (though moderately-windy conditions continued for several more days).||In the mid-upper levels, the main driver behind the fierce winds continued to be a quasi-stationary and powerful/deep closed low churning over the Wisconsin area. At the surface, a nearly co-located intense low pressure center resided over WI during the day, although its central pressure slowly weakened from around 994 millibars (mb) to around 1000 mb. Despite the gradual weakening of this primary surface low, if anything, conditions within the state of Kansas were even more favorable for high winds than the previous day. For one thing, the mid-day (18Z) west-to-east pressure gradient across the state was slightly stronger, registering around 13 mb (ranging from 1012 mb in far northeast KS to 1025 mb far southwest). Furthermore, steep low-level lapse rates allowed efficient downward momentum transfer from aloft during the daylight hours, with wind speeds in the 850-700 millibar layer averaging 45-55 knots (roughly 5 KT higher than on the 6th). From a broader perspective, the high winds of April 6-7 were only two of several markedly windy days during April 2022 across North Central Kansas, as this likely ended up being the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 in the area (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwesterly speeds near or exceeding 40 MPH were common during the day, along with gusts near 60 MPH at times. A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located in Jewell.

Sustained speeds finally eased down below 30 MPH (and gusts below 45 MPH) on a consistent basis by around 9 p.m. CDT on the 7th, signaling the end of back-to-back days of higher-end winds (though moderately-windy conditions continued for several more days).||In the mid-upper levels, the main driver behind the fierce winds continued to be a quasi-stationary and powerful/deep closed low churning over the Wisconsin area. At the surface, a nearly co-located intense low pressure center resided over WI during the day, although its central pressure slowly weakened from around 994 millibars (mb) to around 1000 mb. Despite the gradual weakening of this primary surface low, if anything, conditions within the state of Kansas were even more favorable for high winds than the previous day. For one thing, the mid-day (18Z) west-to-east pressure gradient across the state was slightly stronger, registering around 13 mb (ranging from 1012 mb in far northeast KS to 1025 mb far southwest). Furthermore, steep low-level lapse rates allowed efficient downward momentum transfer from aloft during the daylight hours, with wind speeds in the 850-700 millibar layer averaging 45-55 knots (roughly 5 KT higher than on the 6th). From a broader perspective, the high winds of April 6-7 were only two of several markedly windy days during April 2022 across North Central Kansas, as this likely ended up being the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 in the area (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained northwesterly speeds near or exceeding 40 MPH were common during the day based on nearby observations, along with gusts near 60 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense winds (from varying directions) and widespread blowing dust due to recent very dry conditions were common within North Central Kansas on this Tuesday, driven by the passage of a potent surface low pressure system and accompanying frontal boundaries. The day started out with elongated surface low pressure extending from northeast Colorado northward through the Panhandle of Nebraska and into southwestern South Dakota. A warm front extended eastward along the western/central Nebraska-Kansas border before dipping southward through eastern Kansas, while a dryline was set up across western Kansas. Through the daytime hours, the surface low shifted northeast, pulling the warm front north through the region and pushing the dryline east. By midday the warm front had shifted north of the local area, with the dryline through central Nebraska and Kansas and a strong cold front steadily approaching the western edges of the local area. During the late afternoon and early evening this cold front surged through North Central Kansas from northwest-to-southeast. ||During the morning hours gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the surface dryline, and behind its passage winds shifted more southwesterly. Once the cold front pushed through later in the afternoon and into the early evening, the combination of cold air advection and stout pressure rises fostered frequent west-northwesterly gusts of 50-55 MPH, as well as a handful exceeding 60 MPH. The peak gust for the day in this six-county area was 61 MPH...clocked just before 8 p.m. CDT by a mesonet near Jewell. A bit later and behind the cold front, the Smith Center airport gusted to 60 MPH. This high wind event was one of several during April 2022 within North Central Kansas, as this likely ended up being the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 locally (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 61 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located in Jewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced between 4 and 6 inches of rainfall leading to flooding across southeastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Second Cow Creek flooded 200th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as a band of moderate snowfall that moved over Sheridan County. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 6 to 9.7 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed near the town of Angelus, Kansas. Majority of the heavy snow occurred in the southern half of the county. A few snow reports from the evening hours on the 9th also mention localized blowing snow with near white out conditions due to northeast winds gusting up to 26 mph and the moderate snowfall rate.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swath of heavy snow occurred over portions of north central Kansas on March 9th and 10th. Snow developed just after sunrise on March 9th, initially along the I-80 corridor in south central Nebraska, then slowly shifted southward throughout the day, arriving along the state line during the mid to late afternoon hours. Air temperatures were well below normal for early March, which allowed snow to readily accumulate from the onset and begin to cause slick roads by the evening of the 9th. Snow intensity increased during the evening hours, especially for areas along the Highway 36 corridor where a slow moving band of moderate to heavy snow developed and persisted overnight. Snow intensities only gradually decreased during the morning hours of the 10th. Despite normal high temperatures in the 40s, actual air temperatures remained cold throughout the event in the 20s, which is actually near or below normal LOW temperatures. This led to a primarily dry and fluffy type of snowfall. Total snowfall accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common for Phillips, Smith, and Jewell counties. However, much greater amounts occurred in Rooks, Osborne, and Mitchell counties where 7 to 13 inches of snow fell. For these areas, this would end up as the biggest single event snowfall of the season. Impacts typical of an event of this magnitude, such as slick roads and closures of local schools and businesses, occurred on the 9th and 10th.|This snowfall was forced primarily by persistently moderate to strong frontogenesis in the 700mb to 500mb layer amidst fast, zonal upper flow and a weak upper level disturbance. Moisture availability was pretty limited with this system, however, the aforementioned cold temperatures led to a relatively efficient snowfall, with both duration and high snow to liquid ratios contributing to the heavy snow amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts ranging from 7 to 12 inches were reported across the county. Some of the highest totals included 11 inches from two miles north-northwest of Hunter, 8.2 inches from ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, 7.2 inches from nine miles northeast of Victor, and 7.0 inches from both Beloit and Glen Elder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich gulf moisture streamed north of a slow moving low pressure for the afternoon and overnight hours of May 4th into the early morning of May 5th, 2022.  Heavy rainfall impacted a large portions of southern Kansas, with some flooding reported.  Rainfall amounts of 4 to 5 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire department in Elk City, Kansas, reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 4.70 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely unstable airmass was in place across southern Kansas on May 31st as storms erupted along a stationary front from western Oklahoma into southern Kansas. Storms quickly became severe producing very large hail along with a couple brief tornadoes. In addition, they also produced very heavy rain that caused flash flooding along with subsequent river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown occurred in an open field.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were at least 30 road closures and vehicle submersions with people trapped in cars in the Wichita area. One was described as floating near east 47th and Greenwood. The heavy rain continued into the early morning hours of June 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this six-county North Central Kansas area missed out on the more significant coverage of severe thunderstorms that impacted many places not far off to the north, east and southeast on this Friday afternoon-early evening, it still dealt with spotty strong to severe storms, and more notably a few hours of damaging (mainly non-thunderstorm driven) winds behind a surging dryline/cold front. These damaging winds, which affected various portions of the area and were mainly out of the west/northwest, seemed to be most concentrated within Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties between 5-8 p.m. CDT. During this time, several mesonet stations registered severe-criteria gusts ranging from 58-84 MPH (including 84 MPH seven miles east of Tipton, 79 MPH four miles west-southwest of Beloit, 67 MPH near Osborne and 59 MPH in Plainville). Though ground-truth damage reports were limited, a hay shed was blown down in southeastern Osborne County and at least one large tree was toppled in Beloit. The setup behind these damaging winds was likely a bit complex and multi-faceted, but they were likely driven primarily by a pronounced spike in the local surface pressure gradient between a strong/departing low pressure center and also sharp pressure rises behind the aforementioned surging frontal boundaries. However, this might not have been a purely non-convective wind event either, as many of the enhanced gusts tended to occur in the vicinity of otherwise benign looking rain showers/weak storms, making it plausible that light precipitation falling through a steep low-level lapse rate environment might have also enhanced the ambient gradient-driven winds. Turning to actual strong-to-severe thunderstorms within the local domain, fortunately coverage/impacts were fairly limited and mainly confined to parts of Phillips, Rooks and Smith counties. There was only one report of severe-criteria hail...ping pong ball size near Long Island....as the far southern fringes of a supercell in Nebraska brushed across far northern Phillips County mainly between 5-6 p.m. CDT. During the following hour, a line of storms briefly intensified mainly over Smith/Rooks counties, prompting a 61 MPH gust at the Smith Center airport and dropping hail up to nickel size near Webster State Park. ||Finishing with a closer look at the meteorological situation, overall this was a prime springtime severe weather setup. In the mid-upper levels, a strong, slightly negatively-tilted shortwave trough ejected out of the Rockies and across the heart of Nebraska/northern Kansas over the course of the afternoon-evening. At the surface, the main low pressure center drifted slowly eastward across northern Kansas (peaking at around 992 millibars), with a fairly well-defined warm front taking shape along the KS/NE border ahead of it and a sharp cold front/dryline combination surging in behind it. Within most of the local area, afternoon temperatures soared into the upper 80s-low 90s (F) just south of the warm front, but low-level moisture was seasonably-modest with dewpoints only reaching the 50s ahead of the dryline/cold front. Nonetheless, steep mid-level lapse rates prompted healthy instability with mixed-layer CAPE peaking as high as 1500-2500 J/kg, and deep-layer shear was modestly-strong (as high as 40-50 knots).

In the mid-upper levels, a strong, slightly negatively-tilted shortwave trough ejected out of the Rockies and across the heart of Nebraska/northern Kansas over the course of the afternoon-evening. At the surface, the main low pressure center drifted slowly eastward across northern Kansas (peaking at around 992 millibars), with a fairly well-defined warm front taking shape along the KS/NE border ahead of it and a sharp cold front/dryline combination surging in behind it. Within most of the local area, afternoon temperatures soared into the upper 80s-low 90s (F) just south of the warm front, but low-level moisture was seasonably-modest with dewpoints only reaching the 50s ahead of the dryline/cold front. Nonetheless, steep mid-level lapse rates prompted healthy instability with mixed-layer CAPE peaking as high as 1500-2500 J/kg, and deep-layer shear was modestly-strong (as high as 40-50 knots).

Although this six-county North Central Kansas area missed out on the more significant coverage of severe thunderstorms that impacted many places not far off to the north, east and southeast on this Friday afternoon-early evening, it still dealt with spotty strong to severe storms, and more notably a few hours of damaging (mainly non-thunderstorm driven) winds behind a surging dryline/cold front. These damaging winds, which affected various portions of the area and were mainly out of the west/northwest, seemed to be most concentrated within Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties between 5-8 p.m. CDT. During this time, several mesonet stations registered severe-criteria gusts ranging from 58-84 MPH (including 84 MPH seven miles east of Tipton, 79 MPH four miles west-southwest of Beloit, 67 MPH near Osborne and 59 MPH in Plainville). Though ground-truth damage reports were limited, a hay shed was blown down in southeastern Osborne County and at least one large tree was toppled in Beloit. The setup behind these damaging winds was likely a bit complex and multi-faceted, but they were likely driven primarily by a pronounced spike in the local surface pressure gradient between a strong/departing low pressure center and also sharp pressure rises behind the aforementioned surging frontal boundaries. However, this might not have been a purely non-convective wind event either, as many of the enhanced gusts tended to occur in the vicinity of otherwise benign looking rain showers/weak storms, making it plausible that light precipitation falling through a steep low-level lapse rate environment might have also enhanced the ambient gradient-driven winds. Turning to actual strong-to-severe thunderstorms within the local domain, fortunately coverage/impacts were fairly limited and mainly confined to parts of Phillips, Rooks and Smith counties. There was only one report of severe-criteria hail...ping pong ball size near Long Island....as the far southern fringes of a supercell in Nebraska brushed across far northern Phillips County mainly between 5-6 p.m. CDT. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-upper level trough axis made its way through the Rockies on Friday the 22nd, with a more organized circulation deepening Friday night into early Saturday morning the 23rd as it tracked into the central and northern High Plains. Through the rest of the day on the 23rd this system continued to deepen and become increasingly vertically-stacked, gradually sliding northeast into the central South Dakota/North Dakota border area by that evening. In response to this approaching system, surface low pressure intensified over northeastern Colorado into the southern border of Nebraska/Wyoming on the 22nd, bottoming out around 984 millibars by early evening. With North Central Kansas sandwiched between this surface low and high pressure ridging southward through the entire eastern CONUS, the result was a tightening pressure gradient and intense southerly winds. Sustained southerly speeds of 35-45 MPH and gusts of 45-55 MPH were common across the area by midday on the 22nd.||From early evening and on through the overnight hours, periodic gusts of 55-65 MPH occurred locally, with a peak gust of 65 MPH measured just after 11 p.m. CDT by a mesonet station eight miles north of Bellaire. During the mid-late evening hours, an organized line of thunderstorms developed along the surface cold front over western Nebraska and Kansas, as the main mid-upper level system pushed out of the Rockies into western Nebraska and South Dakota. This activity tracked east through North Central Kansas mainly between 2-6 a.m. CDT on the 23rd. While most of the storms were sub-severe, increasing deep-layer shear and downdraft CAPE values near 1000 J/kg aided in a handful of storms producing severe gusts, including a peak gust of 67 MPH clocked by a mesonet station near Stockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained southerly speeds near or exceeding 40 MPH were common during the day based on nearby observations, along with gusts near 60 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense winds (from varying directions) and widespread blowing dust due to recent very dry conditions were common within North Central Kansas on this Tuesday, driven by the passage of a potent surface low pressure system and accompanying frontal boundaries. The day started out with elongated surface low pressure extending from northeast Colorado northward through the Panhandle of Nebraska and into southwestern South Dakota. A warm front extended eastward along the western/central Nebraska-Kansas border before dipping southward through eastern Kansas, while a dryline was set up across western Kansas. Through the daytime hours, the surface low shifted northeast, pulling the warm front north through the region and pushing the dryline east. By midday the warm front had shifted north of the local area, with the dryline through central Nebraska and Kansas and a strong cold front steadily approaching the western edges of the local area. During the late afternoon and early evening this cold front surged through North Central Kansas from northwest-to-southeast. ||During the morning hours gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the surface dryline, and behind its passage winds shifted more southwesterly. Once the cold front pushed through later in the afternoon and into the early evening, the combination of cold air advection and stout pressure rises fostered frequent west-northwesterly gusts of 50-55 MPH, as well as a handful exceeding 60 MPH. The peak gust for the day in this six-county area was 61 MPH...clocked just before 8 p.m. CDT by a mesonet near Jewell. A bit later and behind the cold front, the Smith Center airport gusted to 60 MPH. This high wind event was one of several during April 2022 within North Central Kansas, as this likely ended up being the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 locally (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the Smith Center Airport AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as a band of moderate snowfall that moved over Graham County. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 6 to 11 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed in Hill City, Kansas. Heaviest snow accumulations occurred from Hill City south while the northern half of the county saw amounts of 6 to 8 inches. With the heavy snow accumulation, several vehicle slide-offs occurred on roads across the county.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through central and south central Kansas during the evening into the overnight hours. These storms produced quarter sized hail and 60 to 70 mph winds. Heavy rainfall associated with the storms led to flash flooding as well in central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a 24 hour storm total of 3.00 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through central and south central Kansas during the evening into the overnight hours. These storms produced quarter sized hail and 60 to 70 mph winds. Heavy rainfall associated with the storms led to flash flooding as well in central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a storm total for the last 24 hours of 3.73 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month the drought status worsened for most of Northwest Kansas.  By the end of the month Rawlins and Decatur counties had fallen to D3 Extreme Drought.  Otherwise the only county not in D2 Severe Drought was Wallace.  Up to four inches of precipitation occurred, with the highest amounts in Norton County.  This was a departure of a half inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of one to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts for the county ranged from 0.25-4.00, with the highest amounts in the north half of the county.  This was an inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of 2.00-3.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month the drought status worsened for Logan, Wichita and Gove counties.  Up to two inches of precipitation occurred, with the highest amounts in Gove County.  This was a departure of a half inch above to two inches below the normal precipitation amounts of half an inch to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts for the county ranged from 0.25-1.50, with the highest amounts in the east half of the county.  This was half an inch above to two inches below the normal precipitation amounts of 1.50-2.00.

These damaging winds, which affected various portions of the area and were mainly out of the west/northwest, seemed to be most concentrated within Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties between 5-8 p.m. CDT. During this time, several mesonet stations registered severe-criteria gusts ranging from 58-84 MPH (including 84 MPH seven miles east of Tipton, 79 MPH four miles west-southwest of Beloit, 67 MPH near Osborne and 59 MPH in Plainville). Though ground-truth damage reports were limited, a hay shed was blown down in southeastern Osborne County and at least one large tree was toppled in Beloit. The setup behind these damaging winds was likely a bit complex and multi-faceted, but they were likely driven primarily by a pronounced spike in the local surface pressure gradient between a strong/departing low pressure center and also sharp pressure rises behind the aforementioned surging frontal boundaries. However, this might not have been a purely non-convective wind event either, as many of the enhanced gusts tended to occur in the vicinity of otherwise benign looking rain showers/weak storms, making it plausible that light precipitation falling through a steep low-level lapse rate environment might have also enhanced the ambient gradient-driven winds. Turning to actual strong-to-severe thunderstorms within the local domain, fortunately coverage/impacts were fairly limited and mainly confined to parts of Phillips, Rooks and Smith counties. There was only one report of severe-criteria hail...ping pong ball size near Long Island....as the far southern fringes of a supercell in Nebraska brushed across far northern Phillips County mainly between 5-6 p.m. CDT. During the following hour, a line of storms briefly intensified mainly over Smith/Rooks counties, prompting a 61 MPH gust at the Smith Center airport and dropping hail up to nickel size near Webster State Park. ||Finishing with a closer look at the meteorological situation, overall this was a prime springtime severe weather setup. In the mid-upper levels, a strong, slightly negatively-tilted shortwave trough ejected out of the Rockies and across the heart of Nebraska/northern Kansas over the course of the afternoon-evening. At the surface, the main low pressure center drifted slowly eastward across northern Kansas (peaking at around 992 millibars), with a fairly well-defined warm front taking shape along the KS/NE border ahead of it and a sharp cold front/dryline combination surging in behind it. Within most of the local area, afternoon temperatures soared into the upper 80s-low 90s (F) just south of the warm front, but low-level moisture was seasonably-modest with dewpoints only reaching the 50s ahead of the dryline/cold front. Nonetheless, steep mid-level lapse rates prompted healthy instability with mixed-layer CAPE peaking as high as 1500-2500 J/kg, and deep-layer shear was modestly-strong (as high as 40-50 knots).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located two miles north of Glen Elder.

Intense winds (from varying directions) and widespread blowing dust due to recent very dry conditions were common within North Central Kansas on this Tuesday, driven by the passage of a potent surface low pressure system and accompanying frontal boundaries. The day started out with elongated surface low pressure extending from northeast Colorado northward through the Panhandle of Nebraska and into southwestern South Dakota. A warm front extended eastward along the western/central Nebraska-Kansas border before dipping southward through eastern Kansas, while a dryline was set up across western Kansas. Through the daytime hours, the surface low shifted northeast, pulling the warm front north through the region and pushing the dryline east. By midday the warm front had shifted north of the local area, with the dryline through central Nebraska and Kansas and a strong cold front steadily approaching the western edges of the local area. During the late afternoon and early evening this cold front surged through North Central Kansas from northwest-to-southeast. ||During the morning hours gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the surface dryline, and behind its passage winds shifted more southwesterly. Once the cold front pushed through later in the afternoon and into the early evening, the combination of cold air advection and stout pressure rises fostered frequent west-northwesterly gusts of 50-55 MPH, as well as a handful exceeding 60 MPH. The peak gust for the day in this six-county area was 61 MPH...clocked just before 8 p.m. CDT by a mesonet near Jewell. A bit later and behind the cold front, the Smith Center airport gusted to 60 MPH. This high wind event was one of several during April 2022 within North Central Kansas, as this likely ended up being the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 locally (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located one mile northwest of Jewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as a band of moderate snowfall that moved over Thomas County. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed in Colby, Kansas. Majority of the county was reported to receive 4 to 6 inches. A few snow reports from the evening hours on the 9th also mention localized blowing snow with visibility down to 1 mile due to northeast winds gusting up to 28 mph and the moderate snowfall rate.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, blowing snow, slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich gulf moisture streamed north of a slow moving low pressure for the afternoon and overnight hours of May 4th into the early morning of May 5th, 2022.  Heavy rainfall impacted a large portions of southern Kansas, with some flooding reported.  Rainfall amounts of 4 to 5 inches were common.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An airman at McConnell Air Force base was impacted by a lightning strike near him on the flight line.  He was injured and taken to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the month the drought status worsened for most of Northwest Kansas.  By the end of the month Rawlins and Decatur counties had fallen to D3 Extreme Drought.  Otherwise the only county not in D2 Severe Drought was Wallace.  Up to four inches of precipitation occurred, with the highest amounts in Norton County.  This was a departure of a half inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of one to three inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation amounts for the county ranged from 0.01-0.50.  This was an inch to three inches below the normal precipitation amounts of 2.00-3.00.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a 24 hour storm total of 3.64 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was truly a one storm show within this North Central Kansas area on this Thursday evening, as a large, very slow-moving supercell presided over much of the northern half of Phillips County for about four hours, causing flooding rains, large hail and even sparking a few brief, lightning-induced grass fires along its leading (southern) edge in advance of the heavy rain core. Above all else, flash flooding/areal flooding was the main impact. Though ground-truth gauge reports were very limited, radar data strongly supported that much of the northern half of the county received a widespread 2-4 inches of rain, with particularly some north central locales likely registering at least 4-6 (there was even an unofficial report of 9 in the bullseye of heaviest rain). True flash flooding subsided before sunrise on Friday the 29th, but emergency management reported that a number of rural gravel roads had been (or were still) covered with water, particularly within a zone 5-10 miles north of Phillipsburg. In addition, a CoCoRaHS observer six miles east of Phillipsburg reported early on the 29th that a nearby creek (likely Big Creek) was in flood due to the deluge several miles upstream (merely 0.12 fell at his residence). Besides flooding, large hail was the main impact from this storm, as there were a handful of reports of stones ranging from 1-2 diameter. These large hail reports originated from as far north as Highway 183 near the Nebraska border, as far west as the Prairie View area and as far southeast as roughly seven miles east-northeast of Phillipsburg. ||Breaking down event evolution, this supercell initiated about 20 miles north of the state line in Harlan County Nebraska around 4 p.m. CDT, within an axis of modest surface moisture convergence along a weak trough axis extending northeastward out of western Kansas.  However, given an extremely slow southward storm motion, its leading edge did not reach far northern Phillips County until around 6:30 p.m. CDT. Over the next few hours it crept slowly south through northern portions of the county before becoming nearly stationary particularly between 9-10 p.m. CDT. Finally, between 10-10:30 p.m. CDT this storm steadily weakened and dissipated, but not before reversing course and making a last minute shift back north toward the state line. Although the Phillips County supercell was clearly the weather story of the night within North Central Kansas, several hours later (mainly between 2:30-5:30 a.m. CDT on the 29th) scattered storms flared up over much of the area before departing off to the north/east. Radar data suggested that a few marginally-severe hail cores might have briefly occurred with this activity (mainly in Phillips/Mitchell counties), but there were no verifying reports. In the mid-upper levels, this was a weakly-forced event as North Central Kansas resided under fairly benign quasi-zonal flow (even subtle weak ridging), well-downstream from a larger-scale trough that remained well to the west during the evening (over the Intermountain West). As a result, deep-layer wind shear was not overly-strong, averaging around 35 knots. In addition, low-level moisture was seasonably-modest, with surface dewpoints peaking only mid-50s (F). However, due in part to afternoon temperatures climbing a bit higher than anticipated (highs mid-80s), mixed-layer CAPE topped out as high as 1500-2000 J/kg. Ultimately, the combination of healthy instability, modest shear and eventually an increasing low level jet proved sufficient to sustain the Phillips County supercell for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size was reported along this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was truly a one storm show within this North Central Kansas area on this Thursday evening, as a large, very slow-moving supercell presided over much of the northern half of Phillips County for about four hours, causing flooding rains, large hail and even sparking a few brief, lightning-induced grass fires along its leading (southern) edge in advance of the heavy rain core. Above all else, flash flooding/areal flooding was the main impact. Though ground-truth gauge reports were very limited, radar data strongly supported that much of the northern half of the county received a widespread 2-4 inches of rain, with particularly some north central locales likely registering at least 4-6 (there was even an unofficial report of 9 in the bullseye of heaviest rain). True flash flooding subsided before sunrise on Friday the 29th, but emergency management reported that a number of rural gravel roads had been (or were still) covered with water, particularly within a zone 5-10 miles north of Phillipsburg. In addition, a CoCoRaHS observer six miles east of Phillipsburg reported early on the 29th that a nearby creek (likely Big Creek) was in flood due to the deluge several miles upstream (merely 0.12 fell at his residence). Intense winds (from varying directions) and widespread blowing dust due to recent very dry conditions were common within North Central Kansas on this Tuesday, driven by the passage of a potent surface low pressure system and accompanying frontal boundaries. The day started out with elongated surface low pressure extending from northeast Colorado northward through the Panhandle of Nebraska and into southwestern South Dakota. A warm front extended eastward along the western/central Nebraska-Kansas border before dipping southward through eastern Kansas, while a dryline was set up across western Kansas. Through the daytime hours, the surface low shifted northeast, pulling the warm front north through the region and pushing the dryline east. By midday the warm front had shifted north of the local area, with the dryline through central Nebraska and Kansas and a strong cold front steadily approaching the western edges of the local area. During the late afternoon and early evening this cold front surged through North Central Kansas from northwest-to-southeast. ||During the morning hours gusty southerly winds developed ahead of the surface dryline, and behind its passage winds shifted more southwesterly. Once the cold front pushed through later in the afternoon and into the early evening, the combination of cold air advection and stout pressure rises fostered frequent west-northwesterly gusts of 50-55 MPH, as well as a handful exceeding 60 MPH. The peak gust for the day in this six-county area was 61 MPH...clocked just before 8 p.m. CDT by a mesonet near Jewell. A bit later and behind the cold front, the Smith Center airport gusted to 60 MPH. This high wind event was one of several during April 2022 within North Central Kansas, as this likely ended up being the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 locally (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located one mile west of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout April 2022, at least portions of five of the six North Central Kansas counties were assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor as a steadily-worsening late-winter/early-spring drought situation peaked in intensity, driven by significant precipitation/snowfall deficits since late-fall 2021. Exacerbating the situation were relentless winds, as this was in fact the overall-windiest April since at least 1996 in North Central Kansas (per analysis of data from nearby Concordia/Hill City airports). Impacts-wise, although the local area did not endure massive wildfires such as those that occurred in nearby south central/southwest Nebraska, the combination of increasingly-parched soils and relentless winds prompted an above average occurrence of blowing dust on several days. April marked the third-straight month with D2 assigned to portions of the area. The entirety of Rooks County spent April in D2, along with large parts of Phillips County and smaller sections of Osborne, Smith and Mitchell counties. The remainder of the area, including all of Jewell County, was categorized as Moderate Drought (D1). ||Turning to April 2022 precipitation data, over 90 percent of the six-county domain tallied at least slightly below normal monthly precipitation (mainly less than 2.30 inches). Of greater concern, roughly three-quarters of the area received less than half its normal (mainly less than 1.15) and at least 20 percent of it less than one-fourth its normal (mainly less than 0.60). The very-driest places of all concentrated within Mitchell, Osborne and west-central Phillips counties, including NWS/CoCoRaHS observer totals of merely 0.18 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, 0.37 in Logan and 0.41 in Natoma. The only small section of the local area to feature above normal April precipitation was within northern Phillips County, largely stemming from a heavy rain event on the 28th that dumped 3-6 (and localized higher) in just a few hours. To gain fuller perspective on the magnitude of the longer-term drought situation, precipitation trends over the six-month span from Nov. 2021-April 2022 told a more complete story. During this time, not only did most of North Central Kansas receive less than one-half of normal precipitation, but some places checked in closer to one-third of normal. To list a few of the very-driest official NWS observer precipitation totals over these six months: 1) Smith Center: 2.61 (38% of normal...9th-driest on record out of 113 years)...2) Logan 2.61 (41% of normal...6th-driest of 71 years)...3) Beloit 2.98 (38% of normal...9th-driest of 116 years).

On May 19th, a cold front passed through the northwestern region of Kansas. Due to this cold front, winds gusted up to around 62 mph causing blowing dust that lowered visibility down to near zero miles within portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports received via social media of visibility down to near zero due to blowing dust in western portions of Thomas county near the towns of Levant and Brewster. The nearby KCBK AWOS measured wind gusts up to around 50 mph at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 9th, a cyclone developed over Colorado and moved southeast into Southwest Kansas during the evening hours. This cyclone produced two broad bands of snow that moved over Northwest Kansas, creating snow accumulations ranging from 3.5 to 13 inches across the area through the morning hours on the 10th. Counties south of Interstate 70 received the heaviest accumulations of 5 to 13. During the morning hours of the 9th, two large areas of light snow developed. One near the Colorado, Kansas, Nebraska state lines generally impacted the counties of Cheyenne, Rawlins, and Sherman counties before developing into a moderate snow band that moved south of the interstate during the afternoon. The other large area developed generally along and east of U.S. Highway 83 in Decatur, Norton, Sheridan, Graham, and Gove counties before moving east of the area. During the event snowfall rate along with NE winds with peak gusts of 25 to 30 mph created blowing snow conditions that caused reduced visibilities as low as one mile at times. Multiple slide offs and accidents also occurred due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced light snow as well as a band of moderate snowfall that moved over Wallace County. Snow accumulations across the county ranged from 7 to 13 inches, with the heaviest snow accumulations observed in Sharon Springs and Wallace, Kansas. A few snow reports from the evening hours on the 9th also mention localized blowing snow with reduced visibility to an unknown distance due to northeast winds gusting up to 25 mph and the moderate snowfall rate. Snow-packed roads were also reported across the county.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads, blowing snow, slide offs.

Numerous vehicle slide-offs were reported on Interstate 70 and across other county roadways due to the snow accumulations.  Key impacts:  snow packed roads,  slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich gulf moisture streamed north of a slow moving low pressure for the afternoon and overnight hours of May 4th into the early morning of May 5th, 2022.  Heavy rainfall impacted a large portions of southern Kansas, with some flooding reported.  Rainfall amounts of 4 to 5 inches were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Maple City, Kansas, reported a 24 hour total of 4.19 inches.
