EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely unstable airmass was in place across southern Kansas on May 31st as storms erupted along a stationary front from western Oklahoma into southern Kansas. Storms quickly became severe producing very large hail along with a couple brief tornadoes. In addition, they also produced very heavy rain that caused flash flooding along with subsequent river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred in an open field based on a video sent via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was truly a one storm show within this North Central Kansas area on this Thursday evening, as a large, very slow-moving supercell presided over much of the northern half of Phillips County for about four hours, causing flooding rains, large hail and even sparking a few brief, lightning-induced grass fires along its leading (southern) edge in advance of the heavy rain core. Above all else, flash flooding/areal flooding was the main impact. Though ground-truth gauge reports were very limited, radar data strongly supported that much of the northern half of the county received a widespread 2-4 inches of rain, with particularly some north central locales likely registering at least 4-6 (there was even an unofficial report of 9 in the bullseye of heaviest rain). True flash flooding subsided before sunrise on Friday the 29th, but emergency management reported that a number of rural gravel roads had been (or were still) covered with water, particularly within a zone 5-10 miles north of Phillipsburg. In addition, a CoCoRaHS observer six miles east of Phillipsburg reported early on the 29th that a nearby creek (likely Big Creek) was in flood due to the deluge several miles upstream (merely 0.12 fell at his residence). Besides flooding, large hail was the main impact from this storm, as there were a handful of reports of stones ranging from 1-2 diameter. These large hail reports originated from as far north as Highway 183 near the Nebraska border, as far west as the Prairie View area and as far southeast as roughly seven miles east-northeast of Phillipsburg. ||Breaking down event evolution, this supercell initiated about 20 miles north of the state line in Harlan County Nebraska around 4 p.m. CDT, within an axis of modest surface moisture convergence along a weak trough axis extending northeastward out of western Kansas.  However, given an extremely slow southward storm motion, its leading edge did not reach far northern Phillips County until around 6:30 p.m. CDT. Over the next few hours it crept slowly south through northern portions of the county before becoming nearly stationary particularly between 9-10 p.m. CDT. Finally, between 10-10:30 p.m. CDT this storm steadily weakened and dissipated, but not before reversing course and making a last minute shift back north toward the state line. Although the Phillips County supercell was clearly the weather story of the night within North Central Kansas, several hours later (mainly between 2:30-5:30 a.m. CDT on the 29th) scattered storms flared up over much of the area before departing off to the north/east. Radar data suggested that a few marginally-severe hail cores might have briefly occurred with this activity (mainly in Phillips/Mitchell counties), but there were no verifying reports. In the mid-upper levels, this was a weakly-forced event as North Central Kansas resided under fairly benign quasi-zonal flow (even subtle weak ridging), well-downstream from a larger-scale trough that remained well to the west during the evening (over the Intermountain West). As a result, deep-layer wind shear was not overly-strong, averaging around 35 knots. In addition, low-level moisture was seasonably-modest, with surface dewpoints peaking only mid-50s (F). However, due in part to afternoon temperatures climbing a bit higher than anticipated (highs mid-80s), mixed-layer CAPE topped out as high as 1500-2000 J/kg. Ultimately, the combination of healthy instability, modest shear and eventually an increasing low level jet proved sufficient to sustain the Phillips County supercell for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from pennies up to two inches in diameter was reported along and near this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and moved across northeast Kansas producing sporadic wind gusts very heavy rains and one tornado.  The tornado was rated EF1 with an intermittent path of around 9 miles.  Very heavy rains also produced local flash flooding due to storm training over the same areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least some portion of March 2022, all six counties in this North Central Kansas area were completely-or-partially assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor as a longer-term drought situation continued, marked by significant precipitation/snowfall deficits since late-fall 2021. March marked the second-straight month with D2 assigned to at least a part of the area. Going into more county-based drought category detail, while D2 had already been introduced to southern portions of Rooks/Osborne counties in mid-February, the areal coverage of D2 was significantly expanded in early-March to include all of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, along with primarily southern portions of Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties. During the latter half of the month, a couple of much-needed precipitation events that brought 1-2 (cumulative) to much of the area prompted a one-category improvement to Moderate Drought (D1) across much of especially Osborne, Mitchell and southern Smith counties, but the month ended with D2 still in place across all of Rooks and decent portions of Phillips and Osborne counties. Although agricultural impacts of this worsening early-spring 2022 drought situation were held to a relative minimum due to occurring outside the growing season, the presence of very dry vegetation and lack of snow cover led to an uptick in wildfire activity. A particularly large wildfire scorched parts of northern Jewell County on the afternoon of March 29th, consuming approximately 12,000 acres.  ||Turning to March 2022 precipitation data for the six-county area, it was actually an encouraging month overall, as near-to-slightly-above normal totals generally between 1.50-2.50 prevailed. However, despite these positive trends, the March precipitation only put a partial dent in longer-term deficits that had been mounting since especially November 2021. More specifically, the five month stretch from Nov. 2021-March 2022 only featured around half of normal precipitation across most of North Central Kansas, with some of the driest spots within Smith, Phillips and Mitchell counties. As just a few examples of official cumulative Nov-March precipitation totals from NWS cooperative observers: 1) Smith Center: 2.11 (45 percent of normal)...2) Logan: 2.24 (55% of normal)...3) Beloit: 2.52' (48% of normal).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the area with enough instability to spark off a couple of isolated thunderstorms mainly producing wind gusts up to around 50 mph. There was one gust that went into the severe threshold hitting 64 mph late in the evening after sunset. No other reports were passed on during this isolated event.

A mid-level shortwave trough along with an enhanced meso-scale banding of precipitation produced a narrow area of heavy and very wet snow across the Hwy 50 to Hwy 283 corridors seeing 3-5 inches. Less than 2 inches were observed for locations south and west with the biggest impact being with the strong northerly winds restricting visibility to less than a mile in some locations. Wind gusts early in the afternoon measured 60-65 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed on highway 50 mile marker post 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong gradient ahead of an approaching cold front gave way to gusty southwest winds pushing 50-60 mph with a handful of reports over the 58 mph threshold for the high wind warning that was issued across much of the CWA. This occurred during peak heating of the afternoon before isolated thunderstorms developed across south central Kansas producing small hail under the severe limits for far reaches of Barber County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed at a location 4.4 NE of Hayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercells produce very large hail over portions of north central Kansas the evening of May 14th. Isolated thunderstorms developed along Highway 283 in northern Kansas around 3 PM CDT, then moved east. This activity quickly developed into supercells, with several of these cells ultimately splitting into both left and right movers. Deep layer wind shear on this day favored right-moving supercells to be most dominant. One of the strongest supercells on this day split over Norton County about 3:30 PM CDT, with the right-mover turning southeast into western Phillips County about 30 minutes later. Very large hail was the primary threat, and golf ball to baseball sized hail was reported. This same cell, along with another cell arriving from the west, also tracked across Rooks County while likely maintaining severe strength, but no large hail reports were received. This activity ultimately weakened and dissipated between 5:30-6:30 PM CDT over Rooks and Osborne Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread wind/dust/fire event impacted SW Kansas Tuesday. Wind gusts of 55-60 mph were common. Extreme fire danger resulted, and this was advertised early over social media and through DSS. A total of 8 hot spot notifications were issued. At least two fires were known to be significant, one across Hodgeman, and a large fire across Pawnee county. Some structural damage has been reported. Many DSS calls and verbal wind forecasts were delivered to these counties, highlighting critical wind shift changes. Strong winds also created blowing dust and reduced visibility, with law enforcement in Liberal reporting a multiple vehicle accident in the dust. Peak wind gusts measured included 63 mph at Hayne, 58 mph at Bloom, and 59 mph at Liberal and Pratt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire broke out southwest of Hanston and eventually consumed approximately 1,075 acres.  Several outbuildings were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to above normal precipitation occurred for Sherman and Cheyenne counties in March.  This helped improve the D2 Severe Drought status by the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from one to three inches. This is up to an inch above normal. Normal precipitation amounts for March range from 1-2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms across Kansas and Missouri the evening of June 7th, 2022, continued to evolve into a complex of strong to severe thunderstorms as they moved into the Kansas City Metro area between 12:30 and 1 AM CDT. These storms resulted in numerous reports of wind damage across the region as well as four confirmed tornadoes, one through the southern side of the Kansas City Metro, one in northeastern Jackson County Missouri and two near Louisburg, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the first of the two Miami county tornadoes. It developed along the trailing end of the larger line of thunderstorms moving across the Kansas City metro area. This tornado damaged the Louisburg Cider Mill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant severe weather event narrowly misses north central Kansas, bringing widespread thunderstorms, but only a single severe weather report on May 12th. A strong storm system brought multiple rounds of thunderstorms to the region on this day, with most of the severe weather occurring over south central Nebraska and points north. Please refer to the Storm Data entry for this event for Nebraska for more detailed information. The only severe weather report in north central Kansas came with the first round of thunderstorms during the early afternoon. Large hail up to ping pong ball size was reported in northwest Phillips County before this activity shifted north by around 2PM CDT. A second line of thunderstorms developed over Smith and Osborne Counties around 4PM. Severe thunderstorm warnings were issued for this activity, but no severe reports were received. This activity shifted northeast and east of the area shortly after 6PM. The third, and final, round of thunderstorms began to affect Rooks County around 7PM, and moved across the remainder of north central Kansas through the late evening hours. Similar to earlier in the day, some Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued, but no severe reports were received. Most of the strong activity ended by 10PM, though weak thunderstorms continued until around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball size hail was reported in Prairie View.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a band of snow to the area beginning during the evening hours of March 10th. The snow band continued overnight before gradually coming to an end from north to south during the early morning hours of March 11th. Snowfall generally ranged from around 2 to 5 inches, with the highest accumulations across northern Bourbon County. Roads became snow covered during the evening into early morning hours, particularly during periods of moderate to heavy snow rates. Otherwise, road impacts became more minimal during the daylight hours on March 11th as snow ended. Numerous school closures took place on March 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of snow moved through during the evening hours of March 10th into the early morning hours of March 11th. Roads became snow covered. The Devon CoCoRaHs station KS-BB-17 reported 5.0 inches of total snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front pushed through during the afternoon and evening. A mid-level shortwave trough moving across the plains helped to enhance lift behind the front and produced snow, some of which was heavy.  The higher amounts observed were associated with meso-scale forcing with the a band across northern counties observing 5-10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5.0 inches was observed at Dighton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave trough along with an enhanced meso-scale banding of precipitation produced a narrow area of heavy and very wet snow across the Hwy 50 to Hwy 283 corridors seeing 3-5 inches. Less than 2 inches were observed for locations south and west with the biggest impact being with the strong northerly winds restricting visibility to less than a mile in some locations. Wind gusts early in the afternoon measured 60-65 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6.5 inches was observed at a location 3 NE of Garfield.  Strong winds and the wet snow took down several power poles in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and moved across northeast Kansas producing sporadic wind gusts very heavy rains and one tornado.  The tornado was rated EF1 with an intermittent path of around 9 miles.  Very heavy rains also produced local flash flooding due to storm training over the same areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Weather Service surveyed the area and found a intermittent narrow damage path to outbuildings and a few structures.  Maximum winds estimated to around 97 mph based on the damage indicators and degree of damage.  This was an EF1 tornado.  No injuries occurred due to this tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter measured 6 inches of snow approximately 1 mile west of Abilene, Kansas from a band of heavy snow and fell across much of Dickinson County.  Anywhere from 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the county between 2pm and 6pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least some part of May 2022, at least portions of five of the six North Central Kansas counties were assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), marking the fourth consecutive month with D2 within the area. That being said, for much of the area it was a somewhat encouraging month for drought trends, with prevailing above normal rainfall in most (but not all) places prompting a slight decrease in overall drought intensity over the course of the month. Specifying more county-based categorical detail, May began with at least portions of five counties in D2: Rooks (the entire county), Phillips, Osborne and Mitchell (the remainder of these counties opened the month in Moderate Drought/D1). Meanwhile, Jewell was the only local county to open May in D1-only. Once May got underway, a few much-needed early month rain events quickly started whittling away at drought severity. Already by the USDM update valid May 3rd, D2 was completely removed from Smith County, most of Phillips and parts of Rooks/Osborne (although a sliver of D2 held on in far western Phillips). Thereafter, these categories held pretty firm through most of the remainder of the month, but another round of appreciable late-month rains prompted even more improvement. This time around, D2 was completely removed from Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, and parts of Mitchell, Jewell and Osborne counties improved to D0 (Abnormally Dry)...marking the first appearance of D0 in North Central Kansas since a brief/localized stint in late-March.||Turning to May 2022 precipitation data, the best news of all was that roughly 80 percent of the six county area registered at least slightly above normal, and most of the limited locations that fell short still received at least 75 percent of normal (a big improvement versus April 2022 trends). The majority of North Central Kansas tallied modestly above normal monthly amounts between 4-6 inches. On the wettest side of things, parts of primarily Mitchell County tallied at least 6-8. On the other end of the rain gauge, the only part of the area that fell somewhat significantly short of normal was a portion of southwestern Rooks County, where radar estimation/AHPS analysis suggested only 2-3 fell (no ground-truth gauge readings). Per a few dozen NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, a few of the very-wettest May totals featured 8.90 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit and 7.44 at Glen Elder. In contrast, the overall-driest sums originated from Rooks/Phillips counties, including 3.13 at Webster Dam and 3.28 in Plainville.

Mainly between 10:30 p.m and 12:30 a.m. CDT on this late Sunday night/very early Monday morning, a small complex of severe thunderstorms charged northeastward out of southwest Kansas and across portions of this North Central Kansas area, resulting in reports of hail up to golf ball size and wind gusts to around 70 MPH. The brunt of severe weather occurred in Smith County, with the town of Lebanon suffering some structural and tree damage due to estimated wind gusts near 70 MPH. Hail ranging in size from quarters to golf balls was also reported in eastern portions of the county, south of Bellaire and in Lebanon. Estimated wind gusts near 60 MPH were also reported in Jewell County near Esbon and in Mankato.||Upper level flow across the region was southwesterly on the late evening of the 12th, with broad ridging extending north roughly along the Missouri River, while a stronger trough was working inland through the Pacific Northwest. At the surface, low pressure was in place over eastern Colorado, with an eastward extending warm front across central portions of Kansas during the afternoon-early evening hours. With time through the evening, this front drifted into northern Kansas, remaining there through the overnight hours. A shortwave disturbance embedded in the southwesterly upper level flow drove the development of late afternoon thunderstorms over southwestern Kansas, moving northeast through the evening hours. Sustained by healthy warm air advection and convergence along the nose of a 40-50kt low-level jet, this complex of storms first entered North Central Kansas into Rooks County around 10:30 p.m. CDT. This activity had no shortage of instability, with most-unstable CAPE values exceeding 4000 J/kg, but deep layer shear was lacking, with 0-6km values around 25-30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to isolated severe thunderstorms rumbled across portions of this six-county North Central Kansas areas during the late afternoon and evening hours on this Thursday. The most intense storm in the local area impacted Mitchell County, resulting in a few reports of marginally-severe winds and hail up to ping pong ball size. A gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station six miles west-southwest of Beloit, while a gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located near Tipton. Large hail ranging in size from quarters to ping pong balls was also reported near the towns of Tipton and Victor.||Mid-upper level flow across the region was west-southwesterly, with a subtle shortwave disturbance embedded in the mid-levels sparking off thunderstorms over southwestern Kansas by mid-afternoon. At the surface, elongated low pressure was set up over eastern Colorado northward into the Dakotas, with a warm frontal boundary extending east across the northern half of Kansas. The environment was favorable for the development of supercell storms, with deep layer and effective shear near 40 knots and MLCAPE values of at least 2500 J/kg. There was a concern for the potential for tornadoes in the general region, as 0-1km shear values increased with time to near 30 kts, mainly centered over the I-70 corridor just south of this local area. As scattered thunderstorms spread into the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours, the primary tornadic supercell indeed remained barely south of this coverage area, crossing through Russell and Lincoln counties. However, another supercell eventually developed slightly to its north, pushing slowly east through portions of Osborne and Mitchell counties in this local area mainly between 7-10 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located six miles west-southwest of Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Drought status remained unchanged for Northwest Kansas through November.  The D2 Severe Drought encompassed western parts of Cheyenne, Sherman, and Logan counties, but included nearly all of Wallace, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  The D3 Extreme Drought included all or part of the rest of the counties in Northwest Kansas.  D4 Exceptional Drought extended south into Rawlins and Decatur counties, and north into Logan and Gove counties.  Precipitation for the month ranged from zero to half an inch.  This was near normal to as much as an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from 0.10-0.25.  This an inch below to near normal.  Normal precipitation amounts range from 0.50-1.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercells produce very large hail over portions of north central Kansas the evening of May 14th. Isolated thunderstorms developed along Highway 283 in northern Kansas around 3 PM CDT, then moved east. This activity quickly developed into supercells, with several of these cells ultimately splitting into both left and right movers. Deep layer wind shear on this day favored right-moving supercells to be most dominant. One of the strongest supercells on this day split over Norton County about 3:30 PM CDT, with the right-mover turning southeast into western Phillips County about 30 minutes later. Very large hail was the primary threat, and golf ball to baseball sized hail was reported. This same cell, along with another cell arriving from the west, also tracked across Rooks County while likely maintaining severe strength, but no large hail reports were received. This activity ultimately weakened and dissipated between 5:30-6:30 PM CDT over Rooks and Osborne Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported along this path.

Sustained by healthy warm air advection and convergence along the nose of a 40-50kt low-level jet, this complex of storms first entered North Central Kansas into Rooks County around 10:30 p.m. CDT. This activity had no shortage of instability, with most-unstable CAPE values exceeding 4000 J/kg, but deep layer shear was lacking, with 0-6km values around 25-30 knots.

This Sunday afternoon-evening featured two rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms within western portions of the North Central Kansas area, both of which primarily impacted a swath running through southern Phillips, much of Rooks and far western Osborne counties. The first round, which played out between 2-7 p.m. CDT, was by far the most significant as an initially slow-moving supercell that initiated along the Phillips-Rooks County line gradually evolved into a persistent multicell cluster, dumping torrential rainfall that prompted flash flooding/flooding (especially within north central/northeastern Rooks County) and also scattered pockets of large hail up to 2 inches in diameter. The Rooks County heavy rain/flooding was the biggest story of the day, as limited gauge readings and radar estimation confirmed that roughly the northeast quadrant of the county was drenched with a widespread 2-5, including a mesonet-measured 4.39 in Stockton (the majority of which fell in 90 minutes). As a result, Stockton was at the epicenter of local flooding issues, with several city streets inundated and impassable for up to a few hours (including Highway 183), many nearby rural county roads damaged and also several area basements flooded. Adding insult to injury, the Stockton area was also pummeled by a sustained period of large hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size, breaking numerous windows in homes and vehicles, damaging siding/roofs etc. In addition to the short term flash flooding, several area creeks and rivers also experienced flooding. Official gauges that breached minor flood stage included the Bow Creek near the Rooks/Phillips County line (crested at 10.2 feet on the evening of the 5th...flood stage 9 ft.) and the South Fork Solomon River near Woodston (crested 14.3 ft. on the morning of the 6th...flood stage 12 ft.). Getting back to large hail reports during the first round of convection (and outside the immediate Stockton area), stones ranging from 1-2 diameter were also reported in or near the following towns: Plainville, Zurich, Palco and Speed. By around 7 p.m. CDT the last of the Round 1 storms departed the local area to the south, ushering in a few hours of quiet conditions. However, by 9-10 p.m. CDT Round 2 began developing over much of the same area, flaring up along the southern fringes of a convective complex approaching from southwestern/south central Nebraska. Between 11 p.m. and midnight CDT radar data suggested that a storm with potential quarter size hail traversed parts of eastern Rooks/southwestern Osborne counties, but there were no verifying reports. Up to around 1 of additional rain accompanied this second round, but fortunately it was more progressive than Round 1 and did little to exacerbate any flooding still ongoing. By midnight CDT Round 2 also departed off to the south, ending the severe storm threat for the night within North Central Kansas. Despite the various reports of hail and flooding rains, there were no verified instances of severe-criteria winds (a mesonet in Stockton peaked at 54 MPH during Round 1). ||In the mid-upper levels, this event was only modestly-forced, as the region resided under broad west-northwest flow containing various low amplitude disturbances, but with increasing/respectable deep layer wind shear around 40 knots. There were no notable surface features in play, only a subtle quasi-stationary front draped in west-east fashion near the Kansas-Nebraska border. The initial supercell that kicked off Round 1 around 2 p.m. CDT developed within the eastern periphery of a mixed-layer CAPE gradient (values locally 1000-2000 J/kg) and plume of steeper mid level lapse rates centered over western KS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the CWA late afternoon and into the evening hours. Gusty southwest winds up to 40-50 mph ahead of it with gusts out of the northwest up to 60 mph behind the front. Dust was lifted along the front producing visibilities to less than a mile at times with numerous wildfires as well due to low relative humidity and gusty winds that triggered a red flag warning for most of the CWA.

A strong arctic cold front pushed through the CWA late in the evening on the 21st with wind speed gusts ranging from 50-60 mph. Cold air advection helped to aid in the strong mixing to the surface with temperatures dropping 20-30 degrees within 30 minutes of FROPA. The gradient behind the front was around 10-11mb across the CWA lasting through much of the 22nd of the early morning hours before sunrise. Winds diminished some for the remainder of the day still gusting up to 40-50 mph.

Isolated supercells produce very large hail over portions of north central Kansas the evening of May 14th. Isolated thunderstorms developed along Highway 283 in northern Kansas around 3 PM CDT, then moved east. This activity quickly developed into supercells, with several of these cells ultimately splitting into both left and right movers. Deep layer wind shear on this day favored right-moving supercells to be most dominant. One of the strongest supercells on this day split over Norton County about 3:30 PM CDT, with the right-mover turning southeast into western Phillips County about 30 minutes later. Very large hail was the primary threat, and golf ball to baseball sized hail was reported. This same cell, along with another cell arriving from the west, also tracked across Rooks County while likely maintaining severe strength, but no large hail reports were received. This activity ultimately weakened and dissipated between 5:30-6:30 PM CDT over Rooks and Osborne Counties.

For the first few weeks of June 2022, a small sliver of this six-county North Central Kansas area (parts of far western Phillips County) hung onto Severe Drought (D2), as assigned by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the fifth consecutive month with D2 in at least some portion of the area. Actually, it was a somewhat complex month for drought trends, as many places saw 1-category improvement despite the majority of the area officially measuring at least slightly below normal rainfall (more on this below). Going into county-based USDM drought category details, June opened with most of the six-county area assigned Moderate Drought (D1), except for the aforementioned sliver of D2 in western Phillips County and an area of better-off Abnormally Dry (D0) encompassing much of Mitchell and parts of Jewell/Osborne counties. Thanks to some early-month rains, the first few weeks of June saw the D2 removed from western Phillips County, and also a decent areal reduction of the prevailing D1 (including its removal from all of Rooks County). A drier latter half of the month prompted fewer changes, but there was further limited reduction of D1 mainly in parts of Smith/Jewell counties, while parts of southern Mitchell and southeastern Osborne counties actually became void of all drought categories whatsoever (marking the first occurrence of this within North Central Kansas since Dec. 2021).  ||As mentioned, drought trends were a bit complicated during June, as in some places the categorical improvements seemed to somewhat outpace the raw precipitation data. Focusing on June rainfall, only around one-third of the six-county area technically observed above normal precipitation (generally more than 3.60). However, most of the other roughly two-thirds of the area was only modestly below normal (mainly in the 2.50-3.50 range). Of the limited above normal areas, they were most concentrated within the northeastern half of Rooks County, along with southern Phillips and far western and southern Osborne counties (most places in the 4-6 range). In contrast, the overall-driest locations during June (mainly near to less than 2) were primarily focused within southern Jewell County and northern portions of Mitchell, Phillips and Smith counties. Per a limited number of NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, a few of the driest ground-truth June totals were at Cawker City (1.27), Logan (2.01) and Beloit (2.35), while some of the wettest stations were seven miles north-northeast of Natoma (4.59), four miles west-northwest of Plainville (4.42) and in Phillipsburg (4.11).

Scattered strong to isolated severe thunderstorms rumbled across portions of this six-county North Central Kansas areas during the late afternoon and evening hours on this Thursday. The most intense storm in the local area impacted Mitchell County, resulting in a few reports of marginally-severe winds and hail up to ping pong ball size. A gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station six miles west-southwest of Beloit, while a gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located near Tipton. Large hail ranging in size from quarters to ping pong balls was also reported near the towns of Tipton and Victor.||Mid-upper level flow across the region was west-southwesterly, with a subtle shortwave disturbance embedded in the mid-levels sparking off thunderstorms over southwestern Kansas by mid-afternoon. At the surface, elongated low pressure was set up over eastern Colorado northward into the Dakotas, with a warm frontal boundary extending east across the northern half of Kansas. The environment was favorable for the development of supercell storms, with deep layer and effective shear near 40 knots and MLCAPE values of at least 2500 J/kg. There was a concern for the potential for tornadoes in the general region, as 0-1km shear values increased with time to near 30 kts, mainly centered over the I-70 corridor just south of this local area. As scattered thunderstorms spread into the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours, the primary tornadic supercell indeed remained barely south of this coverage area, crossing through Russell and Lincoln counties. However, another supercell eventually developed slightly to its north, pushing slowly east through portions of Osborne and Mitchell counties in this local area mainly between 7-10 PM CDT.

Mainly between 10:30 p.m and 12:30 a.m. CDT on this late Sunday night/very early Monday morning, a small complex of severe thunderstorms charged northeastward out of southwest Kansas and across portions of this North Central Kansas area, resulting in reports of hail up to golf ball size and wind gusts to around 70 MPH. The brunt of severe weather occurred in Smith County, with the town of Lebanon suffering some structural and tree damage due to estimated wind gusts near 70 MPH. Hail ranging in size from quarters to golf balls was also reported in eastern portions of the county, south of Bellaire and in Lebanon. Estimated wind gusts near 60 MPH were also reported in Jewell County near Esbon and in Mankato.||Upper level flow across the region was southwesterly on the late evening of the 12th, with broad ridging extending north roughly along the Missouri River, while a stronger trough was working inland through the Pacific Northwest. At the surface, low pressure was in place over eastern Colorado, with an eastward extending warm front across central portions of Kansas during the afternoon-early evening hours. With time through the evening, this front drifted into northern Kansas, remaining there through the overnight hours. A shortwave disturbance embedded in the southwesterly upper level flow drove the development of late afternoon thunderstorms over southwestern Kansas, moving northeast through the evening hours. Sustained by healthy warm air advection and convergence along the nose of a 40-50kt low-level jet, this complex of storms first entered North Central Kansas into Rooks County around 10:30 p.m. CDT. This activity had no shortage of instability, with most-unstable CAPE values exceeding 4000 J/kg, but deep layer shear was lacking, with 0-6km values around 25-30 knots.
Small, scattered storms developed early in the AM on 8/3 and produced a few reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree 15 inches in diameter snapped half way up, and corn blown over in a field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small, scattered storms developed early in the AM on 8/3 and produced a few reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole and multiple trees down across the eastern half of Lawrence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered small storms developed again early on 8/4 and produced one damaging wind report in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree downed at N1 Road and multiple smaller limbs also down. Winds estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed along a front late on 8/29 and produced large hail and strong winds in Wabaunsee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed along a front late on 8/29 and produced large hail and strong winds in Wabaunsee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Drought status remained unchanged for Northwest Kansas through November.  The D2 Severe Drought encompassed western parts of Cheyenne, Sherman, and Logan counties, but included nearly all of Wallace, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  The D3 Extreme Drought included all or part of the rest of the counties in Northwest Kansas.  D4 Exceptional Drought extended south into Rawlins and Decatur counties, and north into Logan and Gove counties.  Precipitation for the month ranged from zero to half an inch.  This was near normal to as much as an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from 0.10-0.50.  This was near normal.  Normal precipitation amounts range from 0.50-1.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While this six-county North Central Kansas area missed out on the more impactful severe weather that struck nearby counties in south Central Nebraska along with northeast and northwest Kansas on this Tuesday evening, there were a few marginally-severe wind gusts along the leading edge of a west-east oriented linear complex that tracked through much of the area from northwest-to-southeast between 9:30 p.m. and midnight CDT. The majority of local sensors clocked sub-severe wind gusts up to around 50-55 MPH, but unofficial mesonets measured gusts of 61-63 MPH in the Kirwin and Jewell areas, and estimated 60 MPH winds in Glade downed several tree limbs (this was the only known ground-truth damage). ||This linear system first slipped into the local area into Phillips, Smith and far northern Jewell counties between 9:30-10:00 p.m. CDT after recently developing in south central Nebraska. Over the next few hours, the complex gradually shifted south-southeastward across most of the remainder of North Central Kansas, but fortunately the most intense bowing/QLCS segment anchored to the east side of the line narrowly avoided local counties as it charged out of south central Nebraska into northeast Kansas. By midnight CDT the very back (west) edge of the primary convective line had exited far southern Osborne/Mitchell counties, ending the severe weather threat for the night locally despite the redevelopment of scattered weaker storms a bit later in the night. In the mid-upper levels, west-northwest flow prevailed, featuring a fairly potent (albeit low amplitude) shortwave trough zipping eastward across northern NE/southern SD during the afternoon-evening. At the surface, the day began with a west-east oriented front draped very near the Kansas-Nebraska border, but during the evening this boundary sharpened up and made a more pronounced southward shift through northern/central Kansas in response to a combination of pressure rises driven by the aforementioned upper wave and also convective outflow. In hindsight, North Central Kansas was rather fortunate to avoid a more significant severe weather event given that the evening mesoscale environment was rather ripe, featuring strong deep layer wind shear of at least 50-60 knots and mixed-layer CAPE of 1000-1500 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in some downed tree limbs in Glade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report sent via Facebook of tennis ball sized hail near El Dorado Lake. Time is estimated from radar.

The first round, which played out between 2-7 p.m. CDT, was by far the most significant as an initially slow-moving supercell that initiated along the Phillips-Rooks County line gradually evolved into a persistent multicell cluster, dumping torrential rainfall that prompted flash flooding/flooding (especially within north central/northeastern Rooks County) and also scattered pockets of large hail up to 2 inches in diameter. The Rooks County heavy rain/flooding was the biggest story of the day, as limited gauge readings and radar estimation confirmed that roughly the northeast quadrant of the county was drenched with a widespread 2-5, including a mesonet-measured 4.39 in Stockton (the majority of which fell in 90 minutes). As a result, Stockton was at the epicenter of local flooding issues, with several city streets inundated and impassable for up to a few hours (including Highway 183), many nearby rural county roads damaged and also several area basements flooded. Adding insult to injury, the Stockton area was also pummeled by a sustained period of large hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size, breaking numerous windows in homes and vehicles, damaging siding/roofs etc. In addition to the short term flash flooding, several area creeks and rivers also experienced flooding. Official gauges that breached minor flood stage included the Bow Creek near the Rooks/Phillips County line (crested at 10.2 feet on the evening of the 5th...flood stage 9 ft.) and the South Fork Solomon River near Woodston (crested 14.3 ft. on the morning of the 6th...flood stage 12 ft.). Getting back to large hail reports during the first round of convection (and outside the immediate Stockton area), stones ranging from 1-2 diameter were also reported in or near the following towns: Plainville, Zurich, Palco and Speed. By around 7 p.m. CDT the last of the Round 1 storms departed the local area to the south, ushering in a few hours of quiet conditions. However, by 9-10 p.m. CDT Round 2 began developing over much of the same area, flaring up along the southern fringes of a convective complex approaching from southwestern/south central Nebraska. Between 11 p.m. and midnight CDT radar data suggested that a storm with potential quarter size hail traversed parts of eastern Rooks/southwestern Osborne counties, but there were no verifying reports. Up to around 1 of additional rain accompanied this second round, but fortunately it was more progressive than Round 1 and did little to exacerbate any flooding still ongoing. By midnight CDT Round 2 also departed off to the south, ending the severe storm threat for the night within North Central Kansas. Despite the various reports of hail and flooding rains, there were no verified instances of severe-criteria winds (a mesonet in Stockton peaked at 54 MPH during Round 1). ||In the mid-upper levels, this event was only modestly-forced, as the region resided under broad west-northwest flow containing various low amplitude disturbances, but with increasing/respectable deep layer wind shear around 40 knots. There were no notable surface features in play, only a subtle quasi-stationary front draped in west-east fashion near the Kansas-Nebraska border. The initial supercell that kicked off Round 1 around 2 p.m. CDT developed within the eastern periphery of a mixed-layer CAPE gradient (values locally 1000-2000 J/kg) and plume of steeper mid level lapse rates centered over western KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unofficial personal weather station in Stockton measured 4.39 inches of rain between 2:30-6:00 p.m. CDT, of which 3.29 fell during the initial 90 minutes. Including other rain that fell several hours before/after this main event, the calendar day total for June 5th was 5.38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the CWA late afternoon and into the evening hours. Gusty southwest winds up to 40-50 mph ahead of it with gusts out of the northwest up to 60 mph behind the front. Dust was lifted along the front producing visibilities to less than a mile at times with numerous wildfires as well due to low relative humidity and gusty winds that triggered a red flag warning for most of the CWA.

Mainly between 10:30 p.m and 12:30 a.m. CDT on this late Sunday night/very early Monday morning, a small complex of severe thunderstorms charged northeastward out of southwest Kansas and across portions of this North Central Kansas area, resulting in reports of hail up to golf ball size and wind gusts to around 70 MPH. The brunt of severe weather occurred in Smith County, with the town of Lebanon suffering some structural and tree damage due to estimated wind gusts near 70 MPH. Hail ranging in size from quarters to golf balls was also reported in eastern portions of the county, south of Bellaire and in Lebanon. Estimated wind gusts near 60 MPH were also reported in Jewell County near Esbon and in Mankato.||Upper level flow across the region was southwesterly on the late evening of the 12th, with broad ridging extending north roughly along the Missouri River, while a stronger trough was working inland through the Pacific Northwest. At the surface, low pressure was in place over eastern Colorado, with an eastward extending warm front across central portions of Kansas during the afternoon-early evening hours. With time through the evening, this front drifted into northern Kansas, remaining there through the overnight hours. A shortwave disturbance embedded in the southwesterly upper level flow drove the development of late afternoon thunderstorms over southwestern Kansas, moving northeast through the evening hours. Sustained by healthy warm air advection and convergence along the nose of a 40-50kt low-level jet, this complex of storms first entered North Central Kansas into Rooks County around 10:30 p.m. CDT. This activity had no shortage of instability, with most-unstable CAPE values exceeding 4000 J/kg, but deep layer shear was lacking, with 0-6km values around 25-30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 70 MPH resulted in downed tree limbs in Lebanon, some of which were up to eight inches in diameter. Minor roof damage occurred at a local business, and a livestock shed was damaged at a farm located four miles southeast of town.

The Drought status remained unchanged for Northwest Kansas through November.  The D2 Severe Drought encompassed western parts of Cheyenne, Sherman, and Logan counties, but included nearly all of Wallace, Greeley, and Wichita counties.  The D3 Extreme Drought included all or part of the rest of the counties in Northwest Kansas.  D4 Exceptional Drought extended south into Rawlins and Decatur counties, and north into Logan and Gove counties.  Precipitation for the month ranged from zero to half an inch.  This was near normal to as much as an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the month ranged from 0.00-0.25.  This a half inch to an inch below normal.  Normal precipitation amounts range from 0.50-1.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front pushed through the CWA late in the evening on the 21st with wind speed gusts ranging from 50-60 mph. Cold air advection helped to aid in the strong mixing to the surface with temperatures dropping 20-30 degrees within 30 minutes of FROPA. The gradient behind the front was around 10-11mb across the CWA lasting through much of the 22nd of the early morning hours before sunrise. Winds diminished some for the remainder of the day still gusting up to 40-50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to isolated severe thunderstorms rumbled across portions of this six-county North Central Kansas areas during the late afternoon and evening hours on this Thursday. The most intense storm in the local area impacted Mitchell County, resulting in a few reports of marginally-severe winds and hail up to ping pong ball size. A gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station six miles west-southwest of Beloit, while a gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located near Tipton. Large hail ranging in size from quarters to ping pong balls was also reported near the towns of Tipton and Victor.||Mid-upper level flow across the region was west-southwesterly, with a subtle shortwave disturbance embedded in the mid-levels sparking off thunderstorms over southwestern Kansas by mid-afternoon. At the surface, elongated low pressure was set up over eastern Colorado northward into the Dakotas, with a warm frontal boundary extending east across the northern half of Kansas. The environment was favorable for the development of supercell storms, with deep layer and effective shear near 40 knots and MLCAPE values of at least 2500 J/kg. There was a concern for the potential for tornadoes in the general region, as 0-1km shear values increased with time to near 30 kts, mainly centered over the I-70 corridor just south of this local area. As scattered thunderstorms spread into the area during the late afternoon and early evening hours, the primary tornadic supercell indeed remained barely south of this coverage area, crossing through Russell and Lincoln counties. However, another supercell eventually developed slightly to its north, pushing slowly east through portions of Osborne and Mitchell counties in this local area mainly between 7-10 PM CDT.
Weak shear south of a wind shift boundary was sufficient enough behind it to have up to 40 mph gusts and some wind damage that was reported from an isolated severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was estimated to be around 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of extremely dry weather led to drought conditions worsening during the month of August 2022.  The hardest hit areas were across southern Kansas, where some locations experienced some of the driest summers on record.  Numerous counties across southern Kansas climbed from severe drought (D2) to extreme drought (D3) by the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued across the entire county due to continued well below normal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown occurred in open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through the CWA late afternoon and into the evening hours. Gusty southwest winds up to 40-50 mph ahead of it with gusts out of the northwest up to 60 mph behind the front. Dust was lifted along the front producing visibilities to less than a mile at times with numerous wildfires as well due to low relative humidity and gusty winds that triggered a red flag warning for most of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1/4 mile visibility.

For at least some part of May 2022, at least portions of five of the six North Central Kansas counties were assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), marking the fourth consecutive month with D2 within the area. That being said, for much of the area it was a somewhat encouraging month for drought trends, with prevailing above normal rainfall in most (but not all) places prompting a slight decrease in overall drought intensity over the course of the month. Specifying more county-based categorical detail, May began with at least portions of five counties in D2: Rooks (the entire county), Phillips, Osborne and Mitchell (the remainder of these counties opened the month in Moderate Drought/D1). Meanwhile, Jewell was the only local county to open May in D1-only. Once May got underway, a few much-needed early month rain events quickly started whittling away at drought severity. Already by the USDM update valid May 3rd, D2 was completely removed from Smith County, most of Phillips and parts of Rooks/Osborne (although a sliver of D2 held on in far western Phillips). Thereafter, these categories held pretty firm through most of the remainder of the month, but another round of appreciable late-month rains prompted even more improvement. This time around, D2 was completely removed from Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, and parts of Mitchell, Jewell and Osborne counties improved to D0 (Abnormally Dry)...marking the first appearance of D0 in North Central Kansas since a brief/localized stint in late-March.||Turning to May 2022 precipitation data, the best news of all was that roughly 80 percent of the six county area registered at least slightly above normal, and most of the limited locations that fell short still received at least 75 percent of normal (a big improvement versus April 2022 trends). The majority of North Central Kansas tallied modestly above normal monthly amounts between 4-6 inches. On the wettest side of things, parts of primarily Mitchell County tallied at least 6-8. On the other end of the rain gauge, the only part of the area that fell somewhat significantly short of normal was a portion of southwestern Rooks County, where radar estimation/AHPS analysis suggested only 2-3 fell (no ground-truth gauge readings). Per a few dozen NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, a few of the very-wettest May totals featured 8.90 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit and 7.44 at Glen Elder. In contrast, the overall-driest sums originated from Rooks/Phillips counties, including 3.13 at Webster Dam and 3.28 in Plainville.

Cold front leads to strong to severe thunderstorms for portions of north central Kansas during the evening of May 17th. The first thunderstorms of this event developed over Norton County between 4PM and 5PM CDT. The initial concern was the potential for landspouts, and at least a few were reported just west of the local forecast area. Eventually, between 5PM and 7PM, thunderstorms grew upscale into a broken line as they moved into Phillips County at sub-severe levels. The line developed some organization and small bowing segment between 7:30PM and 8PM as it moved into Osborne County. Severe wind gusts near 60 MPH were measured at a mesonet site near Osborne, and estimated gusts up to 75 MPH were also reported in this area. The apex of the bowing segment exited the Hastings forecast area into central Kansas around 9PM to 10PM. The northern portion of the thunderstorm line extended into Mitchell County, but no severe gusts were measured or reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 58 MPH and 60 MPH were measured by a KSU mesonet station located one mile north-northwest of Osborne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front pushed through the CWA late in the evening on the 21st with wind speed gusts ranging from 50-60 mph. Cold air advection helped to aid in the strong mixing to the surface with temperatures dropping 20-30 degrees within 30 minutes of FROPA. The gradient behind the front was around 10-11mb across the CWA lasting through much of the 22nd of the early morning hours before sunrise. Winds diminished some for the remainder of the day still gusting up to 40-50 mph.

Between the late afternoon of Thursday the 30th and the early morning of Friday July 1st, numerous showers and mainly weak thunderstorms occurred within this six-county North Central Kansas area. Mainly between 6-7 p.m. CDT, a small line of storms briefly became strong to marginally-severe over northern portions of Smith and Jewell counties, with a mesonet station eight miles north of Bellaire measuring a gust of 58 MPH. Heavy rain also affected a small portion of the area. Aided by an increased southwesterly low-level jet during the evening, a very narrow west-east oriented band of moderate to heavy rain set up directly along the Kansas-Nebraska state line, with the brunt of accumulation occurring prior to midnight CDT. Although there were no known gauge readings within the North Central Kansas portion of this heavy rain stripe, radar estimation and a few measurements just over the state line in Superior NE strongly suggest that at least 2-3 inches fell across the extreme northern fringes of Jewell County.||Upper level flow across the Central Plains was westerly, as the region resided on the southern side of a larger scale low pressure system working its way east across Canada. An accompanying area of low pressure at the surface deepened as it shifted east into Ontario during the daytime hours, with a southward trailing cold front sliding through the Northern and Central Plains. By late afternoon, this cold front was draped from eastern NE into west-central KS, working into and stalling over North Central Kansas. Though better overall upper level lift was north of the region, thunderstorms were able to fire along this boundary locally. Mixed-layer CAPE peaked between 1000-2000 J/kg, but deep layer shear was on the lower side, around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located eight miles north of Bellaire.

The D3 Extreme Drought included all or part of the rest of the counties in Northwest Kansas.  D4 Exceptional Drought extended south into Rawlins and Decatur counties, and north into Logan and Gove counties.  Precipitation for the month ranged from zero to half an inch.  This was near normal to as much as an inch below normal.

Specifying more county-based categorical detail, May began with at least portions of five counties in D2: Rooks (the entire county), Phillips, Osborne and Mitchell (the remainder of these counties opened the month in Moderate Drought/D1). Meanwhile, Jewell was the only local county to open May in D1-only. Once May got underway, a few much-needed early month rain events quickly started whittling away at drought severity. Already by the USDM update valid May 3rd, D2 was completely removed from Smith County, most of Phillips and parts of Rooks/Osborne (although a sliver of D2 held on in far western Phillips). Thereafter, these categories held pretty firm through most of the remainder of the month, but another round of appreciable late-month rains prompted even more improvement. This time around, D2 was completely removed from Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, and parts of Mitchell, Jewell and Osborne counties improved to D0 (Abnormally Dry)...marking the first appearance of D0 in North Central Kansas since a brief/localized stint in late-March.||Turning to May 2022 precipitation data, the best news of all was that roughly 80 percent of the six county area registered at least slightly above normal, and most of the limited locations that fell short still received at least 75 percent of normal (a big improvement versus April 2022 trends). The majority of North Central Kansas tallied modestly above normal monthly amounts between 4-6 inches. On the wettest side of things, parts of primarily Mitchell County tallied at least 6-8. On the other end of the rain gauge, the only part of the area that fell somewhat significantly short of normal was a portion of southwestern Rooks County, where radar estimation/AHPS analysis suggested only 2-3 fell (no ground-truth gauge readings). Per a few dozen NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, a few of the very-wettest May totals featured 8.90 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit and 7.44 at Glen Elder. In contrast, the overall-driest sums originated from Rooks/Phillips counties, including 3.13 at Webster Dam and 3.28 in Plainville.

A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A highly visible tornado tracked across portions of Andover, Kansas, during the early evening hours of April 29th, 2022. The tornado went through a highly populated portion of town, including 2 different subdivisions. Also a health club took a direct hit, but no injuries occurred due to actions taken by the health club. Only 1 direct injury occurred from the tornado, with 3 indirect injuries occurred during search and rescue operations. Total damaged house or destroyed structures in Andover and rural Butler County were 205. Of the damaged properties, approximately 180 were residences and the rest were businesses or city buildings. Damage estimates were around $37 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of extremely dry weather led to drought conditions worsening during the month of August 2022.  The hardest hit areas were across southern Kansas, where some locations experienced some of the driest summers on record.  Numerous counties across southern Kansas climbed from severe drought (D2) to extreme drought (D3) by the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate drought (D1) rapidly expanded to severe drought (D2) and eventually extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month, due to well below normal rainfall across the county.  The Airport in Chanute, Kansas only reported 0.87 inches of rainfall for the month.  This was 2.97 inches below normal.  This was the third consecutive month that was 2 inches below normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A highly visible tornado that came to be known as the Andover Tornado initially touched down in rural Sedgwick county and tracked to the east northeast into Butler county, Kansas. Two direct injuries occurred, when a manufactured home was completely destroyed, with two occupants inside. Two people were transported to local hospitals with serious injuries.  Total structures affected were 116, with 22 of those being homes.  The rest of the structures affected were barns, sheds, detached garages, etc. This tornado crossed the county line into Butler County. Damage were estimated at  4.5 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the month of June continued into July allowing for the development of Extreme Drought (D3) across portions of extreme southeastern Kansas. Drought conditions deepened to Severe Drought (D2) by the 19th of the month in Cherokee and far southern Crawford Counties and expanded across region through the month. By the 28th, Severe Drought expanded across southeastern Kansas and Extreme Drought (D3) developing in Cherokee and Crawford Counties due to little to no rainfall occurring through much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened over the region from early July to late July. By the last week of July, the US Drought Monitor had designated over half of the county area as being in Severe (D2) drought, with the far southeastern part of the county in Extreme (D3) Drought. The rainfall received during the last week of the month eased drought conditions mainly over northern portions of the County with improvements shown in the early August Drought Monitor. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited rainfall during the month of June continued into July allowing for the development of Extreme Drought (D3) across portions of extreme southeastern Kansas. Drought conditions deepened to Severe Drought (D2) by the 19th of the month in Cherokee and far southern Crawford Counties and expanded across region through the month. By the 28th, Severe Drought expanded across southeastern Kansas and Extreme Drought (D3) developing in Cherokee and Crawford Counties due to little to no rainfall occurring through much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened over the region from early July to late July. By the last week of July, the US Drought Monitor had designated nearly all of the county area as being in Extreme (D3) drought, with the far northwestern part of the county in Severe (D2) Drought. Limited rainfall received during the last week of the month eased drought conditions mainly over portions of the County with improvements shown in the early August Drought Monitor. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some impacts but ground water levels and reservoirs showed little impacts. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low passed to the south of the CWA supporting a cold front through early in the morning hours. Along and behind the front coupled with the backside, produced 1-5 inches of snow for much of the CWA, with higher amounts near the Hwy 83 corridor where the strongest frontogenesis occurred. Starting before sunrise, snow lasted through the afternoon hours ending from west to east late afternoon and into the evening hours into central and south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2.5 inches was observed in Elkhart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid to late morning, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 2 inches of snow, leading to slick roads across the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -30 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one hour before transitioning to snow. The Columbus COOP observer station reported 2.0 inches of snowfall. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 25 to 35 mph resulting in periodic blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 3rd, a warm front passed through the northwestern region of Kansas which allowed thunderstorms to develop. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts up to an estimated 95 mph as well as hail up to peas in size. Not only did the thunderstorms knock out power due to the damaging winds in Sherman county, but lightning from a thunderstorm was reported to have struck a substation in Decatur County, knocking out power for a good portion of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of power lines down between Rd 13 and 14 on Road 59 due to a thunderstorm wind gust. A hog hut also blown 30 ft. and a T-post was bent by the wind gust. Time is radar estimated and wind speed is estimated based on damage indicators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid to late morning, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 2 inches of snow, leading to slick roads across the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -30 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one hour before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHs observer KS-BB-17 in Devon reported 1.1 inches of snowfall. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 30 to 37 mph across the county resulting in periodic blowing snow. Utility companies also reported up to 1000 power outages across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon to early evening hours, a disturbance moved over the High Plains creating isolated to scattered thunderstorms that produced multiple tornadoes, up to ping pong ball sized hail, and damaging winds with gusts up to 90 mph.  The wind damage occurred in Decatur, Sheridan and Graham counties, with damage to an airport hanger in the city of Hoxie, damage to an outbuilding and utility trailer, and numerous large tree limbs downed. The largest hail was reported in southern Logan County while the strongest winds were reported in Hoxie with the damage to the airplane hanger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a metal garage and a pull trailer were blown over due to strong winds estimated up to 90 mph.

The D3 Extreme Drought for Rawlins and Sheridan expanded into Cheyenne, Decatur, Thomas, Logan, Gove, and Wichita counties by the end of the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month despite some rainfall. Across southeastern Kansas, D3 (Extreme Drought) to D4 (Significant Drought) persisted with little change. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across the eastern portion of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of October. The US Drought Monitor indicated the western half of the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of strong to severe thunderstorms swiped through roughly the southwestern half of Rooks County on this Friday evening, yielding a lone severe-criteria wind report. Isolated to scattered thunderstorm development occurred over northwestern Kansas and far southwestern Nebraska during the late afternoon hours. This activity gradually shifted southeast into western portions of North Central Kansas through the early-mid evening hours, affecting only Rooks County in this six-county coverage area. While slightly sub-severe gusts between 50-55 MPH were measured in the Palco and Stockton areas, the peak gust of the event was 59 MPH as measured by a mesonet station in Plainville.||Upper level flow across the region was west-northwesterly and generally on the weaker side, as the area sat on the north side of high pressure centered over the Four Corners region. Late in the afternoon, the surface map showed low pressure set up over western Kansas, with a trough axis extending north through the Nebraska Sandhills. These storms developed near this surface boundary in a hot and deeply-mixed environment, with steep low-mid level lapse rates owing to afternoon temperatures peaking between 100-105 degrees. Though these storms had plentiful instability to work with (MUCAPE values around 2000-3000 J/kg), deep layer shear was on the lower side around 30-35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located in Plainville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although July 2022 opened with no worse than Moderate Drought (D1) within this six-county North Central Kansas area per the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), unfortunately prevailing below normal rainfall particularly in southern portions of the area resulted in the introduction of Severe Drought (D2) to southwestern Rooks County from the 19th onward. This marked the sixth consecutive month with D2 plaguing some portion of North Central Kansas. Getting into county-based USDM drought category details for the six local counties, July opened with a varied mix of mainly D1 Moderate Drought (primarily northern sections of Phillips, Smith, Jewell and Osborne counties) and D0 Abnormally Dry (most of the remainder of the area), except for parts of southern Mitchell and far southeastern Osborne counties which were void of all categories whatsoever. As the month went along, the trend of wetter conditions in northern portions of the area and drier conditions in southern local counties prompted various one-category changes. In addition to the aforementioned introduction of D2 to southwestern Rooks County, the most notable changes included: 1) D1 improved to D0 across much of mainly Smith/Jewell counties...2) D0 degraded to D1 across the majority of Rooks and western Osborne counties.  ||Focusing on July 2022 rainfall data, it was a somewhat even-split between above and below normal rainfall within the six-county area (normal being roughly 4.25), but approximately 60 percent of the area came out on the drier side, particularly much of Rooks, Osborne, western Mitchell and southern Phillips counties . Meanwhile, above normal amounts were most prevalent within much of Smith, Jewell, northern Phillips and northern/eastern Mitchell counties. Per a limited number of NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, a few of the driest July totals included only 0.79 ten miles west-northwest of Stockton, 1.32 in Plainville and 2.51 near Hunter. On the wetter end of the measuring stick, a few of the highest tallies were found in Ionia (5.57), Formoso (5.12) and six miles northwest of Barnard (4.59).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought one to two hours of freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of December 22nd before precipitation changed over to light snow. A brief band of moderate to heavy snow then moved southeast across the area during the mid to late morning, leading to visibilities as low as one quarter of a mile along with very gusty winds and blowing snow at times. Light snow lingered for another hour or two behind the heavier snow band before coming to an end. The light glaze of ice became covered by generally 1 to 2 inches of snow, leading to slick roads across the area. Numerous schools that weren't already off for the Christmas Holiday cancelled school for both December 22nd and December 23rd.||Brutally cold temperatures and wind chills also settled into the area with and behind the snowfall. Record to near record low temperatures were common on the morning of December 23rd with subzero lows, highs in the single digits above zero, and minimum wind chills as low as -20 to -30 degrees. Cold low temperatures in the single digits above zero and wind chills in the single digits to teens below zero continued into the morning of December 24th, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light glaze of freezing drizzle began in the early morning hours of December 22nd and lasted approximately one hour before transitioning to snow. The CoCoRaHs observer station KS-CR-10 approximately 1 mile northeast of Farlington reported 1.8 inches of snowfall. Roads became completely covered and slick, with reduced visibility during the snowfall due to wind gusts upwards of 35 mph resulting in periodic blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged moderate rainfall occurred over West Central Kansas, beginning in the morning, lasting into late afternoon.  Close to four inches of rainfall was reported near Horace, KS.  The prolonged moderate rainfall caused flooding over the CR 12 and O intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning convection produced numerous thunderstorms across the area. Given the early morning atmosphere there was not much wind damage reported with these storms, but a few isolated wind events occurred just south of Kansas City in Miami and Linn Counties. The worst of the damage was some power poles down near Parker, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 14 power poles down near Parker, Kansas. It was unclear if they were broken or leaned over, or if the powerlines were removed from the poles themselves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon to early evening hours, a disturbance moved over the High Plains creating isolated to scattered thunderstorms that produced multiple tornadoes, up to ping pong ball sized hail, and damaging winds with gusts up to 90 mph.  The wind damage occurred in Decatur, Sheridan and Graham counties, with damage to an airport hanger in the city of Hoxie, damage to an outbuilding and utility trailer, and numerous large tree limbs downed. The largest hail was reported in southern Logan County while the strongest winds were reported in Hoxie with the damage to the airplane hanger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station recorded a 61 mph gust at a height of 10 meters and a 45 mph gust at 2 meters.

Extreme Drought (D3) along with Severe Drought (D2) also further expanded northward over the area.  Areas of southern Kansas continued to report 2 to 3 inches of below normal rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) rapidly expanded to Extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month, due to well below normal rainfall across the county.
