EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening low pressure system coming off the Rockies caused surface pressures to drop around 14 mb in 24 hours. This drop in air pressure created a strong pressure gradient that caused strong winds across the area. The only damage from the winds was the top portions of a communications tower blowing over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 60 mph was recorded at the Mesonet site 2 miles west-northwest of Sharon Springs, KS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout November 2022, portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the tenth consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Although official drought categories actually remained unchanged throughout the month, unfortunately November was the second-straight month with solidly below normal precipitation across the entire area (see below for more Nov. precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics, for the six-county area as a whole, the majority of real estate featured Moderate Drought (D1) for the entirety of November. However, of greater concern were various swaths of Severe Drought (D2) within the four western-most local counties, impacting: 1) much of the northern halves of Rooks/Osborne counties along with most of far southern Phillips/Smith counties...2) a narrow stripe across extreme southern Rooks/Osborne counties...3) a narrow swath of far western Phillips County. On the better-off side of things, and farther east, much of Mitchell and southeastern Jewell counties maintained the Abnormally Dry (D0) category throughout November. ||Turning to November 2022 precipitation details, this was the second-straight very dry month for the entirety of this six-county area, but in light of monthly normals, November was even drier than October. In fact, at least 95 percent of the area measured less than one-half of normal (generally less than 0.57) and at least three-fourths of the domain received LESS THAN ONE-FOURTH OF NORMAL (generally less than 0.29). Overall, the majority of North Central Kansas totaled merely between 0.05-0.35. The overall-driest spots (greatest concentration of 0.10 or less) focused within much of Smith, Phillips and especially Rooks counties. Per NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, some of the paltriest tallies included outright ZERO at Webster Dam (first time in November since 1970), merely a Trace near Plainville (driest Nov. since 2012) and 0.06 at both Kirwin Dam and Logan. On the wetter (but still well below normal) side of things, the primary recipients of at least 0.35 resided within the eastern half of the area (particularly Mitchell County), featuring 0.63 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, 0.59 near Scottsville, 0.39 in Beloit and 0.35 in Lebanon.

Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed over portions of Northwest Kansas and extreme Southwest Nebraska, moving south. As the storms moved across Northwest Kansas, wind gusts up to 70 mph caused damage to a Cottonwood tree and small tents in eastern Graham County. As storms moved over eastern Wichita County, up to penny sized hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought marginally severe thunderstorms to portions of North Central Kansas during the evening hours of September 20th. The strongest activity occurred between 6-9 PM CDT, during which time a couple of marginally severe wind gusts were measured. No wind damage reports were received. Thunderstorms weakened considerably with loss of daytime heating during the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the Phillipsburg Airport AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening low pressure system coming off the Rockies caused surface pressures to drop around 14 mb in 24 hours. This drop in air pressure created a strong pressure gradient that caused strong winds across the area. The only damage from the winds was the top portions of a communications tower blowing over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top two sections of a communication tower were blown down, along with a drum antenna. The tower was 5 years old, in good condition, and 60 feet tall. The exact timing and wind speed of occurrence are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 8th, a lee surface trough set up in eastern Colorado allowing for strong southerly winds during the afternoon hours within northwestern Kansas. While most recorded wind gusts stayed around 50 mph or below, a rogue strong wind gust of 59 mph was recorded within the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust was recorded by a Mesonet station located 2 miles north of Atwood, KS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a 24 hour rainfall report of 3.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least some portion of September 2022, at least parts of four of the six North Central Kansas counties were assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the eighth consecutive month that D2 plagued some part of the local area. Despite the continuation of longer-term dryness/precipitation deficits, September was actually an encouraging month overall, with roughly three-fourths of the area measuring at least slightly above normal rainfall...a welcomed trend in the wake of what had just been a Top-10 driest August on record for much of the area (more on Sept. rainfall details below). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics, September opened with southwestern Rooks County assigned D2, while Moderate Drought (D1) blanketed all of Smith, Phillips and the remainder of Rooks counties, along with most of Osborne and western Jewell counties. Outside of these worst-off areas, the Abnormally Dry (D0) category was present across most of Mitchell, eastern Jewell and far eastern Osborne counties to start the month, while far southern Mitchell County was actually void of all drought categories. Over the course of the month, a dry first half prompted some expansion of especially D2, which at one point covered the majority of Rooks and Osborne counties, along with stripes of far southern Smith and far southern/western Phillips counties. However, some decent rain during the latter half of the month brought subsequent improvements. Most notably, this involved a zone of D2-becoming-D1 across much of especially the southern half of Rooks/Osborne counties, along with D1-becoming-D0 across much of western Jewell and northern Smith Counties. In addition, the drought-free area in Mitchell County expanded to include roughly half of the county by month's end. ||Turning to September 2022 rainfall details, the overall trend was encouraging, with around 75 percent of the area receiving at least slightly above normal totals mainly in the 2.25-4.00 inch range. The overall-highest amounts focused within the northern halves of Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties, along with southern portions of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties. Of the roughly 25% of the area that was drier than normal for the month, the greatest coverage focused within northern Osborne County, parts of Phillips County and northeastern Mitchell/far southeastern Jewell counties. Per a few dozen NWS/CoCoRaHS observers (along with a few automated KS Mesonet stations), a few of the wettest September tallies featured 4.43 in Lebanon, 4.27 two miles north-northwest of Hunter and 3.86 at Lovewell Dam. On the driest side of things, a few of the lowest monthly totals included 1.04 near Osborne, 1.61 six miles east of Phillipsburg and 1.82 in Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region as rainfall remained below seasonal normals. There is some small improvement to the drought during the first week of the month, due to some areas receiving over 2 inches of rainfall. But Exceptional drought (D4) or Extreme drought (D3) remained over most of southern Kansas during the month of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) continued across the county. There was some minor improvement during the beginning of the month due to some above normal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of extremely dry weather, led to the drought continuing across the region for the month of September 2022.  Drought category levels continued to degrade over the area with the US Drought Monitor showing Exceptional Drought (D4) expanding northward and westward across most of south central Kansas. Extreme Drought (D3) along with Severe Drought (D2) also further expanded northward over the area.  Areas of southern Kansas continued to report 2 to 3 inches of below normal rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) rapidly expanded to Exceptional Drought (D4) by the middle of the month across the entire county due to continued well below normal rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late night hours of September 29th, decaying thunderstorms allowed for a heat burst to occur within northwestern Kansas. Temperatures suddenly rose about 10��F and relative humidity values dropped almost 20% while a damaging wind gust of an estimated 75 mph was observed which suggests a heat burst event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via social media of a large branch snapped off of a live tree with several small tree branches downed by a strong wind gust at 9 miles south of St. Francis, KS. A nearby Mesonet site suggests a possible heat burst as temperature rose from 70��F to 80��F and relative humidity values dropped from 41% to 23% around the event time. Event time is estimated by radar imagery and wind gust speed is estimated from damage indicators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The vast majority of regional severe weather on this Thursday afternoon-evening focused slightly to the north of the local area (within south central Nebraska) between 4:30-7:30 PM CDT. However, as a cold front moved into North Central Kansas during the late evening, a single storm managed to pulse back up to severe levels in far western Phillips County between 8:30-9 PM. The lone severe report with this storm was quarter size hail in Prairie View. This storm weakened as it moved south into Rooks County during the late evening hours.

Throughout December 2022, portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), with the coverage of D2 expanding somewhat during the month. This marked the 11th consecutive month that D2 plagued parts of the local area. Precipitation-wise, unfortunately December marked the third straight month with widespread below normal totals. That being said, it was not as severely dry (versus normal) as October-November (see below for more Dec. precipitation details). Focusing on county-level USDM drought category specifics during December, the month opened with most of the six-county area in Moderate Drought (D1). However, various swaths of D2 were found within: 1) much of the northern halves of Rooks/Osborne counties along with most of far southern Phillips/Smith counties...2) a narrow stripe across extreme southern Rooks/Osborne counties...3) a narrow swath of far western Phillips County. On the better-off side of things, and farther east, most of Mitchell and southeastern Jewell counties opened the month in the Abnormally Dry (D0) category. As December wore on, drought categories remained unchanged within the eastern half of the area. However, at mid-month, there was a fairly sizable expansion of D2 within the western half, to now encompass all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a touch more of southern Smith County. ||Turning to December 2022 precipitation details, unfortunately at least 95 percent of the area registered below normal, (although no ground-truth gauges could confirm, AHPS analysis suggested that parts of extreme northern Phillips/Smith counties might have received near to slightly above normal). On a somewhat positive note though, at least it was not as severely dry (versus normal) as the preceding few months. The majority of the six-counties totaled between 0.35-0.55, or 35-60% of normal, with relatively uniform areal coverage. Per NWS/CoCoRaHS observers, some of the driest December totals featured: 0.30 four miles west-northwest of Plainville, 0.32 seven miles north-northeast of Natoma and 0.37 in both Smith Center and Natoma. On the wetter (but still below normal) side of things, a few of the highest amounts featured: 0.63 in Cawker City, 0.58 in Beloit and 0.56 near Hunter.||Finishing with an overall recap of annual 2022 precipitation across this North Central Kansas area, it was little surprise that drought took at least a modest turn for the worse. Although nowhere close to record-dry territory, most spots fell between 5-9 short of 30-year normal precipitation (typically receiving only 65-80% of normal). For most places, it was the overall-driest year since 2012. The overall-driest conditions (greatest concentration of annual totals under 18) were mainly concentrated in western portions of Phillips/Rooks counties (including merely 15.42 at Logan...marking the 7th-driest year on record out of 71).

On the wetter (but still below normal) side of things, a few of the highest amounts featured: 0.63 in Cawker City, 0.58 in Beloit and 0.56 near Hunter.||Finishing with an overall recap of annual 2022 precipitation across this North Central Kansas area, it was little surprise that drought took at least a modest turn for the worse. Although nowhere close to record-dry territory, most spots fell between 5-9 short of 30-year normal precipitation (typically receiving only 65-80% of normal). For most places, it was the overall-driest year since 2012. The overall-driest conditions (greatest concentration of annual totals under 18) were mainly concentrated in western portions of Phillips/Rooks counties (including merely 15.42 at Logan...marking the 7th-driest year on record out of 71).

Throughout December 2022, portions of four of the six North Central Kansas counties continued to be assigned Severe Drought (D2) by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), with the coverage of D2 expanding somewhat during the month. That being said, it was not as severely dry (versus normal) as October-November (see below for more Dec. precipitation details). However, various swaths of D2 were found within: 1) much of the northern halves of Rooks/Osborne counties along with most of far southern Phillips/Smith counties...2) a narrow stripe across extreme southern Rooks/Osborne counties...3) a narrow swath of far western Phillips County. However, at mid-month, there was a fairly sizable expansion of D2 within the western half, to now encompass all of Rooks County, along with roughly the western two-thirds of Osborne County, the southwestern half of Phillips County and a touch more of southern Smith County. ||Turning to December 2022 precipitation details, unfortunately at least 95 percent of the area registered below normal, (although no ground-truth gauges could confirm, AHPS analysis suggested that parts of extreme northern Phillips/Smith counties might have received near to slightly above normal). On the evening of August 15th, a cold front advanced towards Northwest Kansas from the west, then stalled along the Colorado/Kansas border going into August 16th. As a result, slow-moving thunderstorms developed which produced torrential rainfall of up to five inches in the area. Hackberry Creek in Cheyenne County was reported to have flooded with this rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received of Hackberry Creek running out of its banks north of St. Francis. Location and duration of event are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle preceded an Arctic cold front overnight on Thursday December 22nd.  A light glaze of ice formed on roadways before a cold front swept through and caused air temperatures to drop to below zero and produced northwest winds of 25 to 50 mph.  A period of moderate to heavy snow also occurred behind the front causing near blizzard conditions for a short time 1-2 hours early Thursday morning Dec 22nd.  Snowfall measurements were hampered by the strong winds however most areas are estimated to have received 1-3 inches of snow.   Areas of blowing snow persisted due to strong winds and dry snow through the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters measured near 2 inches of snow on top of a glaze of ice.  Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph caused some blowing and drifting of snow while wind chills dropped into the -30 to -40 degree range which were the coldest in nearly 30 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout August 2022, southwestern Rooks County of this six-county North Central Kansas area held onto Severe Drought (D2), while most of the remainder of the area ended up in Moderate Drought (D1) by month's end...according to the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM). This marked the seventh consecutive month with D2 plaguing various parts of North Central Kansas. Unfortunately, following what had been somewhat encouraging precipitation trends in July (particularly for much of Phillips, Smith, Jewell and Mitchell counties), things took a significant turn for the worse in August, with several longtime NWS stations enduring one of their Top-10 driest Augusts on record (more on this below). Focusing on county-specific USDM drought category details, only the aforementioned southwestern Rooks County opened the month in D2. Elsewhere, most of the remainder of the six counties featured a varied mix of D1 and Abnormally Dry (D0), with most of the D1 concentrated within large portions of Rooks, Osborne and Phillips counties. Meanwhile, the overall-best conditions resided over far eastern reaches of the area, where parts of eastern Jewell and Mitchell counties opened the month indicated as drought-free. Through most of August, drought categories actually remained quite stable despite the worsening dryness. However, the final USDM issuance of the month (valid on the 30th) featured an inevitable and widespread one-category degradation across much of the area. This mainly resulted in a significant expansion of D1, which blanketed all of Smith, Phillips and Rooks counties at month's end (except for the D2 in southwestern Rooks), along with most of Osborne and western Jewell counties. Only parts of southern Mitchell County ended August void of any drought categories. ||Turning to August 2022 rainfall details, there was very little good news to be found. The majority of North Central Kansas only received between 0.25-0.75. For most of the area it was a Top-10 driest August. As just sampling of various NWS cooperative observer sites, it was the 2nd-driest on record at Ionia (0.12), 3rd-driest at Smith Center (0.26) and 7th-driest at Beloit (0.49). Across the six-county area as a whole, essentially every bit of it received at least slightly below normal precipitation, around 95 percent saw less than half of normal (generally less than 1.76) and around 75% of the area tallied LESS THAN ONE-FOURTH of normal (generally under 0.88). A few of the very-driest NWS/CoCoRaHS stations featured the aforementioned 0.12 at Ionia, 0.15 seven miles north-northeast of Natoma and 0.20 two miles north-northwest of Hunter. On the wetter (but still solidly below normal) side of things, AHPS precipitation analysis suggested that small parts of mainly far southern Rooks, far southern Mitchell and northern Jewell counties might have tallied as much as around 2, but the highest ground-truth totals included 1.75 ten miles east of Hunter, 1.50 in Natoma and 0.99 in Phillipsburg.

This Sunday was one of the windiest days of fall 2022 within this six-county North Central Kansas area, with most of the afternoon-evening featuring south-southwesterly sustained speeds commonly 30-40 MPH and frequent gusts 45-55 MPH (localized 58+ MPH). Fortunately, the local area managed to avoid any notable wildfire activity (albeit not by much, as two sizable fires ignited within five miles of the state line in far south central Nebraska). However, this area did endure a healthy dose of blowing dust, with at least a few hours of widespread visibility reductions to one mile or less, and more localized reductions to less than one-quarter mile (even near-zero). In tandem with the strong winds, the stage was set for the blowing dust thanks to unseasonably-warm temperatures and very dry conditions, with the majority of North Central Kansas in the midst of longer-term drought ranging from Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2),and with worse drought conditions including Exceptional (D4) noted in the dust's primary southwestern Kansas source region. Although impacts from the strong winds/blowing dust indeed materialized, fortunately the local area was spared the possibility of a more widespread severe thunderstorm threat. Despite roughly the eastern half of the area being assigned a Marginal to Slight Risk of severe storms by SPC, convective development proved to be very spotty/weak overall, with only one (unverified) Severe Thunderstorm Warning issued for parts of Mitchell/Jewell counties. The day's biggest story locally was the blowing dust, which mainly concentrated between 2-6 p.m. CDT and during which time a Blowing Dust Advisory was in effect for all six counties. In addition, a more urgent Dust Storm Warning was issued for parts of Rooks, Osborne, Smith and Phillips counties mainly 2:30-4:00 p.m. CDT. Though ground-truth reports were limited, law enforcement reported near-zero visibility in the Stockton area, mainly resulting from larger-scale dust plumes surging up into the local area from the southwest. Getting back to the strong south/southwest winds themselves...most of the unofficial sensors within North Central Kansas clocked peak gusts of 45-55 MPH. However, there were isolated higher gusts that approached/barely breached High Wind criteria, including: 59 MPH near Beloit and 57 MPH in Plainville and Tipton. There were no known reports of significant wind damage.  ||Concluding with a closer look at the meteorological setup on the 23rd, the main player in the mid-upper levels was an expansive/deep trough slowly shifting from the Intermountain West into the Central Rockies over the course of the day. At the surface, the day began with a 988 millibar low pressure center over northeast CO. By early afternoon, an elongated, southwest-northeast oriented trough axis intensified to 986 millibars from northeast CO to far north central NE, with North Central Kansas firmly in the warm sector and within a tightening surface pressure gradient. This gradient, in tandem with steep low-mid level lapse rates and resultant deep mixing, drove the persistent 45-55 MPH gusts throughout the afternoon and early evening, with a subtle pre-frontal trough causing local winds to average more true southerly east of the Highway 281 corridor and more southwesterly to the west. The deep mixing also allowed temperatures to soar and dewpoints/relative humidity to plummet. High temperatures across most of North Central Kansas topped out in the upper 80s-low 90s (around 25 degrees above normal), while relative humidity values plunged to between 15-25 percent across most of the area during the mid-late afternoon. Strong south-southwest winds persisted after sunset, but by late evening the primary surface low migrated into the eastern Dakotas, allowing a trailing cold front to enter far western portions of the local area...turning winds northwesterly and easing gusts to more manageable levels as it passed. By around 2 a.m. CDT on the 24th, this front finally cleared the far southeastern fringes of North Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by an unofficial mesonet station located five miles south-southeast of Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Sunday was one of the windiest days of fall 2022 within this six-county North Central Kansas area, with most of the afternoon-evening featuring south-southwesterly sustained speeds commonly 30-40 MPH and frequent gusts 45-55 MPH (localized 58+ MPH). Fortunately, the local area managed to avoid any notable wildfire activity (albeit not by much, as two sizable fires ignited within five miles of the state line in far south central Nebraska). However, this area did endure a healthy dose of blowing dust, with at least a few hours of widespread visibility reductions to one mile or less, and more localized reductions to less than one-quarter mile (even near-zero). In tandem with the strong winds, the stage was set for the blowing dust thanks to unseasonably-warm temperatures and very dry conditions, with the majority of North Central Kansas in the midst of longer-term drought ranging from Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2),and with worse drought conditions including Exceptional (D4) noted in the dust's primary southwestern Kansas source region. Although impacts from the strong winds/blowing dust indeed materialized, fortunately the local area was spared the possibility of a more widespread severe thunderstorm threat. Despite roughly the eastern half of the area being assigned a Marginal to Slight Risk of severe storms by SPC, convective development proved to be very spotty/weak overall, with only one (unverified) Severe Thunderstorm Warning issued for parts of Mitchell/Jewell counties. The day's biggest story locally was the blowing dust, which mainly concentrated between 2-6 p.m. CDT and during which time a Blowing Dust Advisory was in effect for all six counties. In addition, a more urgent Dust Storm Warning was issued for parts of Rooks, Osborne, Smith and Phillips counties mainly 2:30-4:00 p.m. CDT. Though ground-truth reports were limited, law enforcement reported near-zero visibility in the Stockton area, mainly resulting from larger-scale dust plumes surging up into the local area from the southwest. Getting back to the strong south/southwest winds themselves...most of the unofficial sensors within North Central Kansas clocked peak gusts of 45-55 MPH. However, there were isolated higher gusts that approached/barely breached High Wind criteria, including: 59 MPH near Beloit and 57 MPH in Plainville and Tipton. There were no known reports of significant wind damage.  ||Concluding with a closer look at the meteorological setup on the 23rd, the main player in the mid-upper levels was an expansive/deep trough slowly shifting from the Intermountain West into the Central Rockies over the course of the day. At the surface, the day began with a 988 millibar low pressure center over northeast CO. By early afternoon, an elongated, southwest-northeast oriented trough axis intensified to 986 millibars from northeast CO to far north central NE, with North Central Kansas firmly in the warm sector and within a tightening surface pressure gradient. This gradient, in tandem with steep low-mid level lapse rates and resultant deep mixing, drove the persistent 45-55 MPH gusts throughout the afternoon and early evening, with a subtle pre-frontal trough causing local winds to average more true southerly east of the Highway 281 corridor and more southwesterly to the west. The deep mixing also allowed temperatures to soar and dewpoints/relative humidity to plummet. High temperatures across most of North Central Kansas topped out in the upper 80s-low 90s (around 25 degrees above normal), while relative humidity values plunged to between 15-25 percent across most of the area during the mid-late afternoon. Strong south-southwest winds persisted after sunset, but by late evening the primary surface low migrated into the eastern Dakotas, allowing a trailing cold front to enter far western portions of the local area...turning winds northwesterly and easing gusts to more manageable levels as it passed. By around 2 a.m. CDT on the 24th, this front finally cleared the far southeastern fringes of North Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported near-zero visibility due to blowing dust in the Stockton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month.  Across southeastern Kansas, D3 (Extreme Drought) to D4 (Significant Drought) persisted with little change. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region through the month of September. The US Drought Monitor indicated the county as being in Significant (D4) Drought. The rainfall received during the month provided little relief of drought conditions allowing conditions to worsen. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw significant impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs seeing impacts as well. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the afternoon of June 24th, a single cell thunderstorm formed in eastern Colorado, near the Colorado and Kansas border. This storm split into two with the right section heading slightly north of east into Greely County, KS while the left section moved north-northwest into Cheyenne County, CO. The Greeley County storm produced hail up to penny size. As these storms began dissipating, a line of thunderstorms formed in northeast Colorado and moved east-northeast. These storms extended from east central Colorado into west central Nebraska. Once the line of developing storms caught up to the remnants of the first storms, the line intensified. Over the next few hours in Kansas these storms produced more penny sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month.  Across southeastern Kansas, D3 (Extreme Drought) to D4 (Significant Drought) persisted with little change. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across the region through the month of September. The US Drought Monitor indicated the county as being in Extreme (D3) Drought. The rainfall received during the month provided little relief of drought conditions. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw increasing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing drought conditions. Monetary losses will be included in future reported when the drought ends and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large high precipitation supercell formed near the Nebraska Kansas border and tracked south during the evening of June 11th producing widespread damaging straight line winds of 80-110 mph along with several small tornadoes.   The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated slow moving storm moved across portions of east central Kansas producing a short lived EF0 tornado and flash flooding in and around the community of Madison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rural roads in and around the community of Madison were impassable.  Also, roads in downtown Madison were inundated with water and not passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated slow moving storm moved across portions of east central Kansas producing a short lived EF0 tornado and flash flooding in and around the community of Madison.

Two small lines of storms moved across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts with damage and large hail up to the size of baseballs. Initially an isolated storm moved into northwest Wallace and Sherman counties from Colorado, producing up to egg sized hail. A line of storms moved southeast into Rawlins and Decatur counties from extreme Southwest Nebraska and continued southeast over portions of Northwest and North-Central Kansas. These storms produced measured wind gusts up to 69 mph along with an estimated gust of 90 mph that was associated with a snapped tree in the town of Lenora, Kansas. These storms also produced the largest hail of the event in southeast Decatur County. Mid-evening, another line of storms developed along a boundary on both sides of the Colorado border. This line of storms moved south along the state line producing up to nickel sized hail and 1.5 inches of heavy rain in the city of Goodland with some street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to golf ball sized hail fell for a duration of thirteen minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 12th, showers and thunderstorms developed along a dry line in northwestern Kansas during the evening hours. Thunderstorms in the area produced pea sized hail and wind gusts up to 63 mph. A microburst also occurred in the region that produced damaging wind gusts up to an estimated 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded a 59 mph thunderstorm wind gust at location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted over most of Northwest Kansas, with D2 Severe and D3 Extreme Drought Status.  Precipitation for the month ranged from a half inch to six inches, with the higher precipitation amounts occurring in the Extreme Drought area.  The precipitation amounts were two inches below to as much as four inches above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought continued for the county.  Precipitation amounts ranged from one inch to six inches, with the highest amounts in the eastern part of the county.  This was two inches below to two inches above normal.  Normal precipitation amounts range from two to four inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought and D3 Extreme Drought continued for the county.  Precipitation amounts ranged from two to six inches, with the highest amounts in the southern part of the county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large high precipitation supercell formed near the Nebraska Kansas border and tracked south during the evening of June 11th producing widespread damaging straight line winds of 80-110 mph along with several small tornadoes.   The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCS that pushed across northern zones produced severe weather. DCAPE values of around 1000 J/kg through this area with ample microburst potential. Another MCS complex developed into central Kansas in Ellis County pushing south into south central Kansas clipping the southeastern counties of the CWA. CAPE values near 1500 J/kg and shear values over 30kts helped to aid with instability in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly dime to nickel sized.

A weak mid level boundary progressed through the southern CWA with sufficient enough instability and shear to go along with the 30-35 temperature dew point spread to produced isolated severe thunderstorms. With weak mid level winds, storms moved slowly south to southwest seeing some decent rain amounts as well.

Two small lines of storms moved across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts with damage and large hail up to the size of baseballs. Initially an isolated storm moved into northwest Wallace and Sherman counties from Colorado, producing up to egg sized hail. A line of storms moved southeast into Rawlins and Decatur counties from extreme Southwest Nebraska and continued southeast over portions of Northwest and North-Central Kansas. These storms produced measured wind gusts up to 69 mph along with an estimated gust of 90 mph that was associated with a snapped tree in the town of Lenora, Kansas. These storms also produced the largest hail of the event in southeast Decatur County. Mid-evening, another line of storms developed along a boundary on both sides of the Colorado border. This line of storms moved south along the state line producing up to nickel sized hail and 1.5 inches of heavy rain in the city of Goodland with some street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail observed.

A shortwave trough helped to amplify this round of storms being more widespread but with greater moisture buoyancy further east. Shear was over 30 knots with the earlier more discreet cells, allowing for some large hail. DCAPE values associated with good outflows allowed for many 60 mph wind gusts and above later in the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated to be at least 60 MPH.

A cluster of storms moved out of Nebraska during the evening of June 7th and evolved into a line of storms that accelerated southeast across central and northeast Kansas. At least 2 small tornadoes occurred along with widespread damaging straight line winds of 60 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage survey noted damage to main grain elevator in town with a sporadic damage path noted to the southeast to trees limbs with lack of access further southeast unable to determine how much further southeast path occurred.  Area primarily fallow field or newly planted corn so few DI to see from ground around end point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One large high precipitation supercell formed near the Nebraska Kansas border and tracked south during the evening of June 11th producing widespread damaging straight line winds of 80-110 mph along with several small tornadoes.   The majority of property damage was done by straight line winds within what was the rear flank downdraft of the supercell which was moving south southwest.  The widespread destructive winds within the RFD often preceded any smaller vortex with the small tornadoes making damage from tornadoes difficult to distinguish from the downbursts and microbursts within the larger RFD swath.   We believe there were at least 5 tornadoes that evening all EF0 or EF1.  Ironically, the magnitude of damage from straight line winds in spots exceeded that of the smaller tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris and several trees down along North Street in Marysville, likely associated with a tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two small lines of storms moved across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts with damage and large hail up to the size of baseballs. Initially an isolated storm moved into northwest Wallace and Sherman counties from Colorado, producing up to egg sized hail. A line of storms moved southeast into Rawlins and Decatur counties from extreme Southwest Nebraska and continued southeast over portions of Northwest and North-Central Kansas. These storms produced measured wind gusts up to 69 mph along with an estimated gust of 90 mph that was associated with a snapped tree in the town of Lenora, Kansas. These storms also produced the largest hail of the event in southeast Decatur County. Mid-evening, another line of storms developed along a boundary on both sides of the Colorado border. This line of storms moved south along the state line producing up to nickel sized hail and 1.5 inches of heavy rain in the city of Goodland with some street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts associated with a thunderstorm moving over US Highway 83 were estimated to be 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two small lines of storms moved across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts with damage and large hail up to the size of baseballs. Initially an isolated storm moved into northwest Wallace and Sherman counties from Colorado, producing up to egg sized hail. A line of storms moved southeast into Rawlins and Decatur counties from extreme Southwest Nebraska and continued southeast over portions of Northwest and North-Central Kansas. These storms produced measured wind gusts up to 69 mph along with an estimated gust of 90 mph that was associated with a snapped tree in the town of Lenora, Kansas. This line of storms moved south along the state line producing up to nickel sized hail and 1.5 inches of heavy rain in the city of Goodland with some street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating hail up to the size of tennis balls was reported for a duration of five minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weak mid-level shortwave trough pushed through the CWA, similar to the day before. Ample DCAPE values up to 1000 J/kg helped produce isolated thunderstorms across west central Kansas along or near the Hwy 83 corridor. Shear values were relatively weak but lapse rates were more marginal allowing for some downburst winds to perpetrate across this area of the CWA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two small lines of storms moved across Northwest Kansas during the evening hours producing thunderstorm wind gusts with damage and large hail up to the size of baseballs. Initially an isolated storm moved into northwest Wallace and Sherman counties from Colorado, producing up to egg sized hail. A line of storms moved southeast into Rawlins and Decatur counties from extreme Southwest Nebraska and continued southeast over portions of Northwest and North-Central Kansas. These storms produced measured wind gusts up to 69 mph along with an estimated gust of 90 mph that was associated with a snapped tree in the town of Lenora, Kansas. These storms also produced the largest hail of the event in southeast Decatur County. Mid-evening, another line of storms developed along a boundary on both sides of the Colorado border. This line of storms moved south along the state line producing up to nickel sized hail and 1.5 inches of heavy rain in the city of Goodland with some street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system approached Kansas on Friday April 29th allowing a sharp dryline to track generally along Interstate 135. Storms first developed across central and northeast Kansas near the warm front, but as the early evening hours continued, a couple of storms fired just east of Wichita. One of these storms produced a strong tornado across the east side of Andover, causing extensive damage. This tornado was eventually rated an EF-3. A total of 6 more tornadoes were produced by other storms across portions of central Kansas all the while, blowing dust was proving to be a major hindrance behind the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a brief touchdown in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 13th, thunderstorms developed in northwestern Kansas during the evening hours associated with a surface trough in the region. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts up to an estimated 90 mph and hail up to quarters in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via social media of a approximately 12 inch diameter large tree limb broken off from a large tree.  Time is estimated from radar and wind speed is estimated from damage indicators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exceptional (D4) Drought conditions improved across portions of southeastern Kansas. Drought conditions continued over most of southeastern Kansas during the month despite some rainfall. Across southeastern Kansas, D3 (Extreme Drought) to D2 (Severe Drought) persisted despite rainfall and improving conditions. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Hay growth was impacted along with reports of crop failures, dry stock ponds and low flows on some streams and rivers. Final reports of damages will be placed in the last month the drought occurred in the counties impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite rainfall towards the end of the month and some improvement across the eastern portion of the county, drought conditions continued across the region through the month of December. The US Drought Monitor indicated the southern half of the county as being in Severe (D2) Drought with the remainder of county in Moderate (D1) Drought. Impacts across the region were primarily felt in larger scale agriculture. Farm and stock ponds saw some continuing impacts with ground water levels and reservoirs showing slight drought condition improvement. Estimated monetary crop losses included in this months report are for drought conditions through December 31st, 2022. Drought conditions continued into 2023 and estimated losses for 2023 will be included beginning January 1st until drought conditions end and loses are assessed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the latter half of the morning on December 2nd, strong, dry southwesterly winds began picking up dirt and dust in Eastern Colorado and Western Kansas. This led to areas of reduced visibility due to the blowing dust across Eastern Colorado, Western Kansas, and extreme Southwest Nebraska. A strong cold front entered Northwest Kansas around 11 AM and quickly moved southeast. Before this cold front moved in, it was already collecting dust along its leading edge, forming a wall of dust resembling a haboob. ||As this cold front swept through the area, it gathered more dust and grew in severity and longevity. Many photos and videos showing a wall of dust a few hundred feet high were sent to the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office in Goodland, Kansas. When the wall of dust moved over an area, visibility would drop to near or less than 0.25 mile with wind gusts near and exceeding 70 mile per hour. Strong northwesterly winds and 0.25-mile visibility behind the initial wall of dust would continue for an hour or longer before subsiding. ||Two multi-vehicle accidents were reported, indirectly caused by this dust storm. The first accident was occurred on I-70 near mile marker 77. This accident caused lane blockage on I-70 and some traffic was diverted, however the interstate never fully shutdown. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this accident. The other accident occurred on US Highway 24, west of Hoxie, Kansas. This accident accounted for the sole fatality of this storm event. A wildfire that burned nearly 2250 acres was also reported in Wallace County just before the dust storm swept through the area. This fire was caused by a power line that was blown over into a grass field. The highest recorded wind gust of the day was in Sharon Springs, Kansas at 75 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hill City, Kansas ASOS recorded a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of December 29th, northwestern Kansas saw rainfall turn to snowfall as temperatures cooled behind a cold front that moved through the area. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 inch up to 5 inches within the region. The snowfall lasted into the afternoon hours.  A vehicle accident was caused by the snowfall on I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow fell across Sherman County producing total snow accumulations ranging from 2 to almost 5 inches across the county. The highest snow accumulation was reported at 13 miles north-northwest of Ruleton, Kansas. The Kansas Highway Patrol reported an accident on Interstate 70 about two miles east of Kanorado, Kansas involving an automobile striking the rear of a semi truck at 4:50 AM MST due to the hazardous driving conditions caused by the the winter weather. The accident resulted in one person seriously injured and transferred to the nearby medical center. Two people were also reported uninjured in the accident. Key Impacts: automobile accidents, transportation delays, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass entered the region on the morning of December 22nd, plunging temperatures to below zero. Strong winds combined with the cold temperatures yielded wind chills between -25 and -40 below zero from roughly 10 am on 12/22 to noon on 12/23. A Wind Chill Warning was in effect across the entire CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass sent temperatures below zero along with strong winds. Minimum wind chills across the region generally ranged from -25 to -40 degrees between roughly 10 am on 12/22 to noon on 12/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the latter half of the morning on December 2nd, strong, dry southwesterly winds began picking up dirt and dust in Eastern Colorado and Western Kansas. This led to areas of reduced visibility due to the blowing dust across Eastern Colorado, Western Kansas, and extreme Southwest Nebraska. A strong cold front entered Northwest Kansas around 11 AM and quickly moved southeast. Before this cold front moved in, it was already collecting dust along its leading edge, forming a wall of dust resembling a haboob. ||As this cold front swept through the area, it gathered more dust and grew in severity and longevity. Many photos and videos showing a wall of dust a few hundred feet high were sent to the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office in Goodland, Kansas. When the wall of dust moved over an area, visibility would drop to near or less than 0.25 mile with wind gusts near and exceeding 70 mile per hour. Strong northwesterly winds and 0.25-mile visibility behind the initial wall of dust would continue for an hour or longer before subsiding. ||Two multi-vehicle accidents were reported, indirectly caused by this dust storm. The first accident was occurred on I-70 near mile marker 77. This accident caused lane blockage on I-70 and some traffic was diverted, however the interstate never fully shutdown. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this accident. The other accident occurred on US Highway 24, west of Hoxie, Kansas. This accident accounted for the sole fatality of this storm event. A wildfire that burned nearly 2250 acres was also reported in Wallace County just before the dust storm swept through the area. This fire was caused by a power line that was blown over into a grass field. The highest recorded wind gust of the day was in Sharon Springs, Kansas at 75 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of blowing dust reducing visibility to less than 0.25 mile were recorded by on and off duty NWS employees, the public, and a trained spotter across Sherman county. The first report was in Kanorado, Kansas at 12:45 PM. 0.25 mile visibility continued to occur for almost 1 hour at the Goodland Weather Forecast Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle preceded an Arctic cold front overnight on Thursday December 22nd.  A light glaze of ice formed on roadways before a cold front swept through and caused air temperatures to drop to below zero and produced northwest winds of 25 to 50 mph.  A period of moderate to heavy snow also occurred behind the front causing near blizzard conditions for a short time 1-2 hours early Thursday morning Dec 22nd.  Snowfall measurements were hampered by the strong winds however most areas are estimated to have received 1-3 inches of snow.   Areas of blowing snow persisted due to strong winds and dry snow through the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP station reported 2 inches of snow on top of a glaze (0.02) of ice.  Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph caused some blowing and drifting of snow while wind chills dropped into the -30 to -40 degree range which were the coldest in nearly 30 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of moderate to heavy snow developed during the early morning hours across south central and southeast Kansas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the latter half of the morning on December 2nd, strong, dry southwesterly winds began picking up dirt and dust in Eastern Colorado and Western Kansas. This led to areas of reduced visibility due to the blowing dust across Eastern Colorado, Western Kansas, and extreme Southwest Nebraska. A strong cold front entered Northwest Kansas around 11 AM and quickly moved southeast. Before this cold front moved in, it was already collecting dust along its leading edge, forming a wall of dust resembling a haboob. ||As this cold front swept through the area, it gathered more dust and grew in severity and longevity. Many photos and videos showing a wall of dust a few hundred feet high were sent to the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office in Goodland, Kansas. When the wall of dust moved over an area, visibility would drop to near or less than 0.25 mile with wind gusts near and exceeding 70 mile per hour. Strong northwesterly winds and 0.25-mile visibility behind the initial wall of dust would continue for an hour or longer before subsiding. ||Two multi-vehicle accidents were reported, indirectly caused by this dust storm. The first accident was occurred on I-70 near mile marker 77. This accident caused lane blockage on I-70 and some traffic was diverted, however the interstate never fully shutdown. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this accident. The other accident occurred on US Highway 24, west of Hoxie, Kansas. This accident accounted for the sole fatality of this storm event. A wildfire that burned nearly 2250 acres was also reported in Wallace County just before the dust storm swept through the area. This fire was caused by a power line that was blown over into a grass field. The highest recorded wind gust of the day was in Sharon Springs, Kansas at 75 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a powerline that caused a grassfire. The boundaries of wildfire were CR Kiowa to CR Rawhide (S to N) and CR 130 to CR 170 (W to E). Roughly 2248 acres were burned in the fire. No injuries, no livestock hurt, and no damaged structures were reported as the fire stayed in grassland and canyons. Highway 25 was closed due to the smoke and the fire. Wallace, Logan, Greeley, and Wichita fire departments responded to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast across East-Central Colorado into Northwest Kansas generating up to four hours of blizzard conditions across Cheyenne and Sherman counties. Snow amounts varied from one to three inches across the two counties. The greatest snow accumulations were across Cheyenne County with three inches reported in Saint Francis, Kansas. High winds up to 60 mph were observed with the blizzard conditions in Sherman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast across East-Central Colorado into Northwest Kansas generating three hours of blizzard conditions across Cheyenne County. There were numerous reports from law enforcement and the Cheyenne County Emergency Manager of visibilities dropping to between a quarter mile to zero miles, mainly along Highway 36. Winds were out of the west-northwest with gusts up to 40 mph. During the event, up to three inches of snow fell with drifts reportedly up to three feet tall in spots. Key impacts: blowing snow and blizzard conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved northeast across East-Central Colorado into Northwest Kansas generating four hours of blizzard conditions across Sherman County. There were numerous reports from an off-duty NWS Employee of near blizzard to blizzard conditions in the city of Goodland, Kansas, with visibilities dropping to between a quarter mile to near-zero miles. Winds were out of the west-northwest with gusts up to 60 mph. The high winds occurred from 11:45 am MT to 12:41 pm MT. During the event, up to three inches of snow fell with drifts reportedly up to three feet tall in spots. Key impacts: blowing snow, high winds, and blizzard conditions.

Before this cold front moved in, it was already collecting dust along its leading edge, forming a wall of dust resembling a haboob. ||As this cold front swept through the area, it gathered more dust and grew in severity and longevity. Many photos and videos showing a wall of dust a few hundred feet high were sent to the National Weather Service Weather Forecast Office in Goodland, Kansas. When the wall of dust moved over an area, visibility would drop to near or less than 0.25 mile with wind gusts near and exceeding 70 mile per hour. Strong northwesterly winds and 0.25-mile visibility behind the initial wall of dust would continue for an hour or longer before subsiding. ||Two multi-vehicle accidents were reported, indirectly caused by this dust storm. The first accident was occurred on I-70 near mile marker 77. This accident caused lane blockage on I-70 and some traffic was diverted, however the interstate never fully shutdown. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this accident. The other accident occurred on US Highway 24, west of Hoxie, Kansas. This accident accounted for the sole fatality of this storm event. A wildfire that burned nearly 2250 acres was also reported in Wallace County just before the dust storm swept through the area. This fire was caused by a power line that was blown over into a grass field. The highest recorded wind gust of the day was in Sharon Springs, Kansas at 75 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports from the public showed 0.25 visibility due to blowing dust.
